**Atlantic City**, sometimes referred to by its initials **A.C.**, is a [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore") [seaside resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") in [Atlantic County](/wiki/Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "Atlantic County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").

Atlantic City comprises the second half of the Atlantic City-[Hammonton](/wiki/Hammonton,_New_Jersey "Hammonton, New Jersey") [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), which encompasses those cities and all of Atlantic County for statistical purposes. Both Atlantic City and Hammonton, as well as the surrounding Atlantic County, are culturally tied to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and constitute part of the larger [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Philadelphia metropolitan area") or [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley"), the nation's [seventh-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") as of 2020.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Located in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") on [Absecon Island](/wiki/Absecon_Island "Absecon Island") and known for its [casinos](/wiki/Casino "Casino"), [nightlife](/wiki/Nightlife "Nightlife"), [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)"), and [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") beaches and [coastline](/wiki/Coastline "Coastline"), the city is prominently known as the "Las Vegas of the East Coast" and inspired the U.S. version of the board game *[Monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly_(game) "Monopoly (game)")*, which uses various Atlantic City street names and destinations in the game.[[28]](#cite_note-28) New Jersey voters legalized casino gambling in Atlantic City in 1976, and the first casino opened two years later. From 1921 to 2004, Atlantic City hosted the [Miss America](/wiki/Miss_America "Miss America") pageant, which later returned to the city from 2013 to 2018.

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city had a population of 38,497,[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020-13)[[14]](#cite_note-LWD2020-14) a decline of 1,061 (−2.7%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 39,558,[[29]](#cite_note-Census2010-29)[[14]](#cite_note-LWD2020-14) which in turn reflected a decrease of 959 (−2.4%) from the 40,517 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The city was incorporated on May 1, 1854, from portions of [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey") and [Galloway Township](/wiki/Galloway_Township,_New_Jersey "Galloway Township, New Jersey").[[31]](#cite_note-Story-31) It is located on Absecon Island and borders [Absecon](/wiki/Absecon,_New_Jersey "Absecon, New Jersey"), [Brigantine](/wiki/Brigantine,_New_Jersey "Brigantine, New Jersey"), Egg Harbor Township, Galloway Township, [Pleasantville](/wiki/Pleasantville,_New_Jersey "Pleasantville, New Jersey"), [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey"), and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | It has been suggested that this article be **[split](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting")** out into a new article titled *[History of Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/History_of_Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "History of Atlantic City, New Jersey")*. ([**Discuss**](/wiki/Talk:Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Talk:Atlantic City, New Jersey")) *(June 2024)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Atlantic_City_Map_1877.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City_Map_1877.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_Map_1877.jpg)

*Atlantic City*, 1877

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Brooklyn_Museum_-_A_High_Tide_at_Atlantic_City_-_William_Trost_Richards_-_overall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_A_High_Tide_at_Atlantic_City_-_William_Trost_Richards_-_overall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_A_High_Tide_at_Atlantic_City_-_William_Trost_Richards_-_overall.jpg)

*A High Tide at Atlantic City*, a painting by [William Trost Richards](/wiki/William_Trost_Richards "William Trost Richards"), now housed in [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

Prior to Atlantic City's founding, the region served as a summer home for the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), a [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribe. While the date of European settlement in present-day Atlantic City is not precisely determined, it is commonly thought that it was in 1783, when Jeremiah Leeds built and occupied a year-round home there.

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In 1850, present-day Atlantic City was developed into a [resort town](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town"). Three years later, in early 1853, it was named Atlantic City.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Because of its location in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"), which hugs the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") between [marshlands](/wiki/Marshlands "Marshlands") and islands, Atlantic City was then viewed by developers as prime real estate and a potential resort town. In 1853, the city's first commercial hotel, the Belloe House, was built at the intersection of Massachusetts and Atlantic Avenues.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The following year, in 1854, the city was incorporated. The same year, train service began on the [Camden and Atlantic Railroad](/wiki/Camden_and_Atlantic_Railroad "Camden and Atlantic Railroad").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Built on the edge of the bay, this served as the direct link of this remote parcel of land with [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), the second-most populous city in the United States at the time and the largest city in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). The same year, construction of the [Absecon Lighthouse](/wiki/Absecon_Lighthouse "Absecon Lighthouse"), designed by [George Meade](/wiki/George_Meade "George Meade") of the [Corps of Topographical Engineers](/wiki/Corps_of_Topographical_Engineers "Corps of Topographical Engineers"), was approved, with work initiated the next year.[[35]](#cite_note-LighthouseHistory-35)

By 1874, almost 500,000 passengers a year were coming to Atlantic City by rail. In *Boardwalk Empire: The Birth, High Times, and Corruption of Atlantic City*, "Atlantic City's Godfather"[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Jonathan Pitney](/wiki/Jonathan_Pitney "Jonathan Pitney"), known as the "Father of Atlantic City",[[37]](#cite_note-37) initially sought to develop Atlantic City as a health resort. Pitney persuaded municipal authorities that a railroad to the beach in Atlantic City would be beneficial. His successful business relationship with Samuel Richards, an entrepreneur and member of what was then the most influential family in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"), was designed to construct the railroad with the first 600 riders, who "were chosen carefully by Samuel Richards and Jonathan Pitney":[[38]](#cite_note-Johnson,_Nelson_2010_30-38)

> After arriving in Atlantic City, a second train brought the visitors to the door of the resort's first public lodging, the United States Hotel. The hotel was owned by the railroad. It was a sprawling, four-story structure built to house 2,000 guests. It opened while it was still under construction, with only one wing standing, and even that wasn't completed. By year's end, when it was fully constructed, the United States Hotel was not only the first hotel in Atlantic City but also the largest in the nation. Its rooms totaled more than 600, and its grounds covered some 14 acres.

The first [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)") was built in 1870 along a portion of the beach in an effort to help hotel owners keep sand out of their lobbies. Businesses were restricted and the boardwalk was removed each year at the end of the peak season.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Because of its effectiveness and popularity, the boardwalk was expanded in length and width, and modified several times in subsequent years.

Prior to the destructive [1944 Great Atlantic Hurricane](/wiki/1944_Great_Atlantic_Hurricane "1944 Great Atlantic Hurricane"), the historic length of the boardwalk was about 7 mi (11 km) and it extended from Atlantic City to [Longport](/wiki/Longport,_New_Jersey "Longport, New Jersey"), through [Ventnor](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey") and [Margate](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The first road connecting the city to the mainland at [Pleasantville](/wiki/Pleasantville,_New_Jersey "Pleasantville, New Jersey") was completed in 1870 and charged a 30-cent toll. Albany Avenue was the first road to the mainland available without a toll.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

By 1878, because of the growing popularity of the city, one railroad line could no longer keep up with demand. Soon, the [Philadelphia and Atlantic City Railway](/wiki/Philadelphia_and_Atlantic_City_Railway "Philadelphia and Atlantic City Railway") was also constructed to transport tourists to Atlantic City. At this point massive hotels like the United States Hotel and Surf House, as well as smaller rooming houses, had sprung up all over town. The United States Hotel took up a full city block between Atlantic, Pacific, Delaware, and Maryland Avenues. These hotels were not only impressive in size, but featured the most up-to-date amenities, and were considered quite luxurious for their time.

In 1883, [salt water taffy](/wiki/Salt_water_taffy "Salt water taffy") was conceived in Atlantic City by David Bradley. The traditional story is that Bradley's shop close to the beach was flooded with ocean water after a storm, soaking his taffy. He sold the "salt water taffy" to a girl, who walked down to the beach to show her friends. Bradley's mother was in the back of the store when the sale was made, and loved the name, giving the candy its name.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_crowd_in_front_of_Blenheim_hotel_1911_re-retouched.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_crowd_in_front_of_Blenheim_hotel_1911_re-retouched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_Boardwalk_crowd_in_front_of_Blenheim_hotel_1911_re-retouched.jpg)

Atlantic City Boardwalk crowd in front of [Marlborough-Blenheim Hotel](/wiki/Marlborough-Blenheim_Hotel "Marlborough-Blenheim Hotel") in 1911

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Boardwalk_Hall%E2%80%94Atlantic_City_coat_of_arms.jpg/250px-Boardwalk_Hall%E2%80%94Atlantic_City_coat_of_arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boardwalk_Hall%E2%80%94Atlantic_City_coat_of_arms.jpg)

*Consilio et prudentia*, Atlantic City's motto, along with its coat of arms on historic [Boardwalk Hall](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall "Boardwalk Hall"), built during [prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), which lasted from 1919 to 1933

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9c/Chalfonte_hotel_brochure013.JPG/250px-Chalfonte_hotel_brochure013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chalfonte_hotel_brochure013.JPG)

[Haddon Hall Hotel](/wiki/Resorts_Casino_Hotel "Resorts Casino Hotel") depicted on a mid-1930s postcard

In the early 20th century, Atlantic City experienced a radical building boom. Many of the modest boarding houses that dotted the boardwalk were replaced with large hotels. Two of the city's most distinctive hotels were the [Marlborough-Blenheim Hotel](/wiki/Marlborough-Blenheim_Hotel "Marlborough-Blenheim Hotel") and the [Traymore Hotel](/wiki/Traymore_Hotel "Traymore Hotel").

In 1902, Josiah White III bought a parcel of land near Ohio Avenue and the boardwalk, where he started construction and built the [Queen Anne style](/wiki/Queen_Anne_style_architecture_in_the_United_States "Queen Anne style architecture in the United States") Marlborough House. The hotel was a success. In 1905, he chose to expand the hotel and bought another parcel of land adjacent to his Marlborough House. In an effort to make his new hotel a source of conversation, White hired the architectural firm of Price and McLanahan. The firm made use of [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete "Reinforced concrete"), a new building material invented by Jean-Louis Lambot in 1848, and Joseph Monier received the patent in 1867. The hotel's Spanish and Moorish themes, capped off with its signature dome and chimneys, represented a step forward from other hotels that had a classically designed influence. White named the new hotel the Blenheim and merged the two hotels into the Marlborough-Blenheim. [Bally's Atlantic City](/wiki/Bally%27s_Atlantic_City "Bally's Atlantic City") was later constructed at this location.

The Traymore Hotel was located at the corner of Illinois Avenue and the boardwalk. Constructed in 1879 as a small boarding house, the hotel grew through a series of uncoordinated expansions. By 1914, the hotel's owner, Daniel White, Josiah White's half-brother, taking a hint from the Marlborough-Blenheim, commissioned the firm of Price and McLanahan to build an even bigger hotel to replace the original wooden structure. Rising 16 stories, the tan brick and gold-capped hotel would become one of the city's best-known landmarks. The hotel made use of ocean-facing hotel rooms by jutting its wings farther from the main portion of the hotel along Pacific Avenue. It was demolished in 1972.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

One by one, additional large hotels were constructed along the boardwalk, including the Brighton, Chelsea, Shelburne, Ambassador, Ritz Carlton, Mayflower, Madison House, and the Breakers. The [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers")-owned Chalfonte House, opened in 1868, and Haddon House, opened in 1869, flanked North Carolina Avenue at the beach end. Over the years, their original wood-frame structures would be enlarged, and even moved closer to the beach. The modern Chalfonte Hotel, eight stories tall, opened in 1904. The modern Haddon Hall was built in stages and was completed in 1929, at eleven stories. By this time, they were under the same ownership and merged into the [Chalfonte-Haddon Hall Hotel](/wiki/Chalfonte-Haddon_Hall_Hotel "Chalfonte-Haddon Hall Hotel"), becoming the city's largest hotel with nearly 1,000 rooms.

By 1930, [The Claridge Hotel](/wiki/The_Claridge_Hotel_(Atlantic_City) "The Claridge Hotel (Atlantic City)"), the city's last large hotel constructed before the casino era, opened its doors. The 480-room Claridge was built by a partnership that included renowned Philadelphia contractor [John McShain](/wiki/John_McShain "John McShain"). At 24 stories and 370 stone (5,200 lb; 2,300 kg) in height, it would become known as the "Skyscraper by the Sea".[[45]](#cite_note-45)

With tourism peaking in the 1920s, the period is often considered by historians to be Atlantic City's golden age. During [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), which was enacted nationally in 1919 and lasted until 1933, much liquor was consumed and gambling regularly took place in the back rooms of nightclubs and restaurants. During Prohibition, [racketeer](/wiki/Racketeer "Racketeer") and political boss [Enoch L. "Nucky" Johnson](/wiki/Enoch_L._Johnson "Enoch L. Johnson") rose to power. Prohibition was largely unenforced in Atlantic City. Because alcohol that had been smuggled into the city with the implicit approval of local officials, it was easily obtained at restaurants and other establishments, and the resort's popularity grew further.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The city then dubbed itself as "The World's Playground".[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Nucky Johnson's income, which reached as much as $500,000 annually, came from the [kickbacks](/wiki/Kickback_(bribery) "Kickback (bribery)") he took on illegal liquor, gambling and prostitution operating in the city, as well as from kickbacks on construction projects.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

During this time, Atlantic City was led by mayor [Edward L. Bader](/wiki/Edward_L._Bader "Edward L. Bader"), known for his contributions to the construction, athletics and aviation of Atlantic City.[[50]](#cite_note-casino-50) Despite opposition, he had Atlantic City purchase the land that became the city's municipal airport and high school football stadium, both of which were later named [Bader Field](/wiki/Bader_Field "Bader Field") in his honor.[[51]](#cite_note-51) He led the initiative, in 1923, to construct the [Atlantic City High School](/wiki/Atlantic_City_High_School "Atlantic City High School") at Albany and Atlantic Avenues.[[50]](#cite_note-casino-50) Bader, in November 1923, initiated a public referendum, during the general election, at which time residents approved the construction of a Convention Center. The city passed an ordinance approving a bond issue for $1.5 million to be used for the purchase of land for Convention Hall, now known as the [Boardwalk Hall](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall "Boardwalk Hall"), finalized on September 30, 1924.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Bader was also a driving force behind the creation of the [Miss America](/wiki/Miss_America "Miss America") competition.[[53]](#cite_note-Good2010-53)

In May 1929, Johnson hosted a [conference](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Conference "Atlantic City Conference") for [organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime") figures from all across America that created a [National Crime Syndicate](/wiki/National_Crime_Syndicate "National Crime Syndicate"). The men who called this meeting were [Masseria family](/wiki/Luciano_crime_family#Masseria "Luciano crime family") [lieutenant](/wiki/Caporegime "Caporegime") [Charles "Lucky" Luciano](/wiki/Charlie_Luciano "Charlie Luciano") and former Chicago [South Side Gang](/wiki/Chicago_Outfit "Chicago Outfit") [boss](/wiki/Crime_boss "Crime boss") [Johnny "the Fox" Torrio](/wiki/Johnny_Torrio "Johnny Torrio"), with heads of the [Bugs and Meyer Mob](/wiki/Bugs_and_Meyer_Mob "Bugs and Meyer Mob"), [Meyer Lansky](/wiki/Meyer_Lansky "Meyer Lansky") and [Benjamin Siegel](/wiki/Bugsy_Siegel "Bugsy Siegel"), being used as muscle for the meeting.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Gangster and businessman [Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone "Al Capone") attended the conference and was photographed walking along the Atlantic City boardwalk with Johnson.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The 1930s through the 1960s were a heyday for nightclub entertainment. Popular venues on the white-populated south side included the [500 Club](/wiki/500_Club "500 Club"), the [Clicquot Club](/wiki/Clicquot_Club "Clicquot Club"), and the [Jockey Club](/wiki/Jockey_Club_(Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey) "Jockey Club (Atlantic City, New Jersey)"). In the [Northside neighborhood](/wiki/Northside,_Atlantic_City "Northside, Atlantic City"), home to African Americans in the racially segregated city, a black entertainment district reigned on Kentucky Avenue.

Four major nightclubs, [Club Harlem](/wiki/Club_Harlem "Club Harlem"), the [Paradise Club](/wiki/Paradise_Club_(Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey) "Paradise Club (Atlantic City, New Jersey)"), [Grace's Little Belmont](/wiki/Grace%27s_Little_Belmont "Grace's Little Belmont"), and [Wonder Gardens](/wiki/Wonder_Gardens "Wonder Gardens"), drew both black and white patrons. During the summer tourist season, jazz and R&B music could be heard into the wee hours of the morning. [Soul food](/wiki/Soul_food "Soul food") restaurants and [ribs](/wiki/Ribs_(food) "Ribs (food)") joints also lined Kentucky Avenue, including [Wash's Restaurant](/wiki/Wash%27s_Restaurant "Wash's Restaurant"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) Jerry's and Sap's.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Civil_Air_Patrol_Marker%2C_Bader_Field%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-Civil_Air_Patrol_Marker%2C_Bader_Field%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Civil_Air_Patrol_Marker,_Bader_Field,_Atlantic_City.jpg)

Marker to the Civil Air Patrol, Bader Field, Atlantic City

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/World_War_II_Memorial_Plaque%2C_Convention_Hall%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-World_War_II_Memorial_Plaque%2C_Convention_Hall%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_War_II_Memorial_Plaque,_Convention_Hall,_Atlantic_City.jpg)

Plaque in Atlantic City for World War II Service

Like many older [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") cities after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Atlantic City became plagued with poverty, crime, corruption, and general economic decline in the mid-to-late 20th century. The neighborhood known as the "Inlet" became particularly impoverished. The reasons for the resort's decline were multi-layered. First, the automobile became more readily available to many Americans after the war. Atlantic City had initially relied upon visitors coming by train and staying for a couple of weeks. The car allowed them to come and go as they pleased, and many people would spend only a few days, rather than weeks. The [advent of suburbia](/wiki/Suburbanization#United_States "Suburbanization") also played a significant role. With many families moving to their own private houses, luxuries such as home air conditioning and swimming pools diminished their interest in flocking to the luxury beach resorts during the hot summer. Finally, the rise of relatively cheap jet airline service allowed visitors to travel to year-round resort places such as [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach,_Florida "Miami Beach, Florida") and the [Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The city hosted the [1964 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1964_Democratic_National_Convention "1964 Democratic National Convention") which nominated [Lyndon Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_Johnson "Lyndon Johnson") for president and [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey") as vice president. The convention and the press coverage it generated, however, cast a harsh light on Atlantic City, which by then was in the midst of a long period of economic decline. Many felt that the friendship between Johnson and [Governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") [Richard J. Hughes](/wiki/Richard_J._Hughes "Richard J. Hughes") led Atlantic City to host the Democratic Convention.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

By the late 1960s, many of the resort's once great hotels were suffering from high vacancy rates. Most of them were either shut down, converted to cheap apartments, or converted to nursing home facilities by the end of the decade. Prior to and during the advent of legalized gambling, many of these hotels were demolished. The Breakers, [The Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_Hotel_(Atlantic_City) "Chelsea Hotel (Atlantic City)"), the Brighton, the Shelburne, the Mayflower, the [Traymore](/wiki/Traymore_Hotel "Traymore Hotel") and the [Marlborough-Blenheim](/wiki/Marlborough-Blenheim_Hotel "Marlborough-Blenheim Hotel") were demolished in the 1970s and 1980s. Of the many pre-casino resorts that bordered the boardwalk, only the Claridge, the Dennis, the [Ritz-Carlton](/wiki/Ritz-Carlton_Hotel_(Atlantic_City) "Ritz-Carlton Hotel (Atlantic City)"), and the Haddon Hall survive to this day as parts of [Bally's Atlantic City](/wiki/Bally%27s_Atlantic_City "Bally's Atlantic City"), a condo complex, and [Resorts Atlantic City](/wiki/Resorts_Atlantic_City "Resorts Atlantic City"). The old Ambassador Hotel was purchased by Ramada in 1978 and was gutted to become the [Tropicana Casino and Resort Atlantic City](/wiki/Tropicana_Casino_and_Resort_Atlantic_City "Tropicana Casino and Resort Atlantic City"), only reusing the steelwork of the original building.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Smaller hotels off the boardwalk, such as the [Madison](/wiki/Madison_Hotel_(Atlantic_City) "Madison Hotel (Atlantic City)") also survived.

#### Legalized gambling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legalized gambling")]

Main article: [Gambling in New Jersey](/wiki/Gambling_in_New_Jersey "Gambling in New Jersey")

In an effort at revitalizing the city, New Jersey voters in 1976 passed a referendum, approving [casino](/wiki/Casino "Casino") gambling for Atlantic City; this came after a 1974 referendum on legalized gambling failed to pass. Immediately after the legislation passed, the owners of the [Chalfonte-Haddon Hall Hotel](/wiki/Chalfonte-Haddon_Hall_Hotel "Chalfonte-Haddon Hall Hotel") began converting it into the Resorts International. It was the first legal casino in the eastern United States when it opened on May 26, 1978.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Other casinos were soon constructed along the Boardwalk and, later, in the marina district for a total of nine today.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The introduction of gambling did not, however, quickly eliminate many of the urban problems that plagued Atlantic City. Many people have suggested that it only served to exacerbate those problems, as attested to by the stark contrast between tourism intensive areas and the adjacent impoverished working-class neighborhoods.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

While Atlantic City has been less popular than [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") as a gambling city in the United States,[[63]](#cite_note-63) other events such as live music, boxing matches, and performances like [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil") became popular at hotel casinos. [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson") fought most of his fights in Atlantic City in the 1980s, which helped Atlantic City achieve national attention as a gambling resort and vacation destination.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Several [highrise condominiums](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Atlantic_City "List of tallest buildings in Atlantic City") were built for use as permanent residences or second homes.[[65]](#cite_note-65) By end of the decade, it was one of the most popular tourist destinations in the United States.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

#### Legalized sports betting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Legalized sports betting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Borgata.jpg/250px-Borgata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borgata.jpg)

[Borgata](/wiki/Borgata "Borgata"), Atlantic City's highest-grossing casino

With the redevelopment of the [Las Vegas Strip](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip") and the opening of [Foxwoods Resort Casino](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") and [Mohegan Sun](/wiki/Mohegan_Sun "Mohegan Sun") in [Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_casinos_in_Connecticut "List of casinos in Connecticut") in the early 1990s, along with newly built casinos in the nearby [Philadelphia metro area](/wiki/List_of_casinos_in_Pennsylvania "List of casinos in Pennsylvania") in the 2000s, Atlantic City's tourism began to decline due to its failure to diversify away from gambling. In 1999 the Atlantic City Redevelopment Authority partnered with Las Vegas casino mogul [Steve Wynn](/wiki/Steve_Wynn "Steve Wynn") to develop a new roadway to a barren section of the city near the Marina. Nicknamed "The Tunnel Project", Steve Wynn planned the proposed 'Mirage Atlantic City' around the idea that he would connect the $330 million tunnel stretching 2.5 mi (4.0 km) from the [Atlantic City Expressway](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Expressway "Atlantic City Expressway") to his new resort. The roadway was later officially named the [Atlantic City-Brigantine Connector](/wiki/Atlantic_City-Brigantine_Connector "Atlantic City-Brigantine Connector"), and funnels incoming traffic off of the expressway into the city's marina district and the city of [Brigantine](/wiki/Brigantine,_New_Jersey "Brigantine, New Jersey").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Although Wynn's plans for development in the city were scrapped in 2002, the tunnel opened in 2001. The new roadway prompted Boyd Gaming in partnership with MGM/Mirage to build Atlantic City's newest casino. [Borgata](/wiki/Borgata "Borgata") opened in July 2003, and its success brought an influx of developers to Atlantic City with plans for building grand, Las Vegas-style mega casinos to revitalize the aging city.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Owing to economic conditions and the [late 2000s recession](/wiki/Late_2000s_recession "Late 2000s recession"), many of the proposed mega casinos never advanced further than the initial planning stage. One of these developers was [Pinnacle Entertainment](/wiki/Pinnacle_Entertainment "Pinnacle Entertainment"), which purchased the [Sands Atlantic City](/wiki/Sands_Atlantic_City "Sands Atlantic City") for $250–$270 million and closed it on November 11, 2006 with plans to replace it with a larger casino.[[69]](#cite_note-parmley-69)[[70]](#cite_note-PoA-70) The following year, the resort was demolished in an implosion, the first of its kind in Atlantic City. While Pinnacle Entertainment intended to replace it with a $1.5-$2-billion casino resort, the company canceled its construction plans and sold the land for $29.5 million.[[70]](#cite_note-PoA-70) [MGM Resorts International](/wiki/MGM_Resorts_International "MGM Resorts International") announced in October 2007 that it would pull out of all development for Atlantic City, effectively ending its plans for the MGM Grand Atlantic City.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In 2006, [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") purchased 20 acres (8.1 ha) directly north of the [Showboat Atlantic City](/wiki/Showboat_Atlantic_City "Showboat Atlantic City") for a new $2-billion-plus casino resort.[[73]](#cite_note-73) [Revel Entertainment Group](/wiki/Revel_Entertainment_Group "Revel Entertainment Group") was named as the project's developer for the [Revel Casino](/wiki/Revel_Casino "Revel Casino"). Revel was hindered with many problems, the biggest setback occurring in April 2010 when Morgan Stanley, the owner of 90% of Revel Entertainment Group, decided to discontinue funding for continued construction and put its stake in Revel up for sale. Early in 2010, the New Jersey state legislature passed a bill offering tax incentives to attract new investors and complete the job, but a poll by [Fairleigh Dickinson University](/wiki/Fairleigh_Dickinson_University "Fairleigh Dickinson University")'s [PublicMind](/wiki/Fairleigh_Dickinson_University#PublicMind "Fairleigh Dickinson University") released in March 2010 showed that 60% of voters opposed the legislation, and two of three of those who opposed it "strongly" opposed it.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) Ultimately, Governor [Chris Christie](/wiki/Chris_Christie "Chris Christie") offered Revel $261 million in state tax credits to assist the casino once it opened.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Revel completed all of the exterior work and had continued work on the interior after finally receiving the funding necessary to complete construction, and had a soft opening in April 2012 before being fully open the next month. Ten months later, in February 2013, after serious losses and a write-down in the value of the resort from $2.4 billion to $450 million, Revel filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. It was restructured but still could not carry on and re-entered bankruptcy on June 19, 2014. It was put up for sale, however as no suitable bids were received the resort closed its doors in September 2014. The property was bought by AC Ocean Walk, LLC for $200 million in 2017, and reopened in 2018 as Ocean Casino Resort.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

On October 29, 2012, "[Superstorm Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy")" struck Atlantic City and caused flooding and power-outages but left minimal damage to any of the tourist areas.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The storm produced an all-time record low barometric pressure reading of 943 mb (27.85") for not only Atlantic City, but the state of New Jersey.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

In the wake of the closures and declining revenue from casinos, Governor Christie said in September 2014 that the state would consider a 2015 referendum to end the 40-year-old monopoly that Atlantic City holds on casino gambling and allowing gambling in other municipalities. With casino revenue declining from $5.2 billion in 2006 to $2.9 billion in 2013, the state saw a drop in money from its 8% tax on those earnings, which is used to fund programs for senior citizens and the disabled.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

In May 2018, the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") ruled in *[Murphy v. National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Murphy_v._National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Murphy v. National Collegiate Athletic Association")* that the [Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act](/wiki/Professional_and_Amateur_Sports_Protection_Act_of_1992 "Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act of 1992") was unconstitutional. The act was overturned, allowing New Jersey to move ahead with plans to implement legalized sports betting.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Despite being the state to initiate the landmark ruling, New Jersey was actually the third state to legalize sports betting after [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"). In June 2018, [New Jersey governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") [Phil Murphy](/wiki/Phil_Murphy "Phil Murphy") signed the legislation into law, and several New Jersey–based casino brands subsequently opened [sportsbooks](/wiki/Sportsbook "Sportsbook"), especially in Atlantic City.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

In 2019, the Atlantic City area had the highest rates of foreclosures in the nation.[[85]](#cite_note-85) This has disproportionately affected Black residents in neighborhoods segregated by [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining"), a legacy that is mirrored by the values of properties on the Monopoly game board.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

After several casino closures and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_Jersey "COVID-19 pandemic in New Jersey"), strikes and pickets were being threatened in June 2022 by casino employees which were short-staffed and wanted pay raises.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Food desert

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Food desert")]

In 2022, the [New Jersey Economic Development Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Economic_Development_Authority "New Jersey Economic Development Authority") ranked Atlantic City second on its list of the state's [food desert](/wiki/Food_desert "Food desert") communities (behind [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey")). Atlantic City was one of a dozen municipalities statewide on the list where the entire municipality was a food desert community.[[89]](#cite_note-89) As of 2024, there was only one functioning supermarket in Atlantic City, the [Save-A-Lot](/wiki/Save-A-Lot "Save-A-Lot") food store located in Renaissance Plaza, an area of the city known for its significant homeless population and drug use. The nearest full-service supermarket being located in neighboring [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey"), which is 3 miles (4.8 km) away from the majority of Atlantic City's population. In an area with unemployment 50% above the statewide average and with 87% of area residents lacking a car, getting access to a supermarket for city residents has been difficult, requiring long rides on public transportation.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

To remedy this, proposals for a supermarket were floated beginning in May 2021, and Atlantic City's City Council authorized the Casino Reinvestment Development Authority to look for developers to build a supermarket. The Casino Reinvestment Development Authority proposed a new supermarket that would be located on an empty parking lot behind the Tanger Outlets and near the [Atlantic City Expressway](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Expressway "Atlantic City Expressway").

Groundbreaking for a [ShopRite](/wiki/ShopRite_(United_States) "ShopRite (United States)") supermarket had taken place in October 2021, after Village Super Market received $18.7 million from the Casino Reinvestment Development Authority to construct the store. The supermarket was expected to be completed by December 2022, although no construction or building had happened on the site. After the time for the contract expired, the Casino Reinvestment Development Authority (CRDA) announced that the deal with Village Super Market was dead and that they were looking for new contenders.[[91]](#cite_note-91) VSM created a new plan for CRDA and the city's Council to review and by July 2023, it and a Chinese conglomerate headquartered in Hong Kong emerged as the two candidates to build the supermarket.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In October 2025, after the effort to address the city's food desert status by constructing the proposed ShopRite had fizzled out, the city announced a $20 million deal to redevelop Renaissance Plaza centered around an expanded Sav-A-Lot supermarket, using funds from the [Casino Reinvestment Development Authority](/wiki/Casino_Reinvestment_Development_Authority "Casino Reinvestment Development Authority") and the [New Jersey Economic Development Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Economic_Development_Authority "New Jersey Economic Development Authority"). The project, expected to be completed by the end of 2026, would increase the size of the existing store by 50% to more than 28,000 square feet (2,600 m2), allowing the expansion of fresh fruit, vegetable and meat options to shoppers.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Atlantic_City_beach_looking_north_at_Chelsea_Avenue.jpeg/250px-Atlantic_City_beach_looking_north_at_Chelsea_Avenue.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_beach_looking_north_at_Chelsea_Avenue.jpeg)

Beach in Atlantic City

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Atlantic City had a total area of 17.21 square miles (44.59 km2), including 10.76 square miles (27.87 km2) of land and 6.45 square miles (16.72 km2) of water (37.50%).[[12]](#cite_note-CensusArea-12)[[20]](#cite_note-GR1-20)

The city is located on 8.1 mi-long (13.0 km) [Absecon Island](/wiki/Absecon_Island "Absecon Island"), along with [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey"), [Margate City](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey") and [Longport](/wiki/Longport,_New_Jersey "Longport, New Jersey") to the southwest.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Atlantic City borders the Atlantic County municipalities of [Absecon](/wiki/Absecon,_New_Jersey "Absecon, New Jersey"), [Brigantine](/wiki/Brigantine,_New_Jersey "Brigantine, New Jersey"), [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey"), [Galloway Township](/wiki/Galloway_Township,_New_Jersey "Galloway Township, New Jersey"), [Pleasantville](/wiki/Pleasantville,_New_Jersey "Pleasantville, New Jersey"), and [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey").[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

The city is located 60 mi (97 km) southeast of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and 125 mi (201 km) south of New York City.

[Unincorporated communities](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey#Unincorporated_communities "Local government in New Jersey"), localities and place names located partially or completely within the city include Chelsea, City Island, Great Island and Venice Park.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Monthly_Climate_Normals_%281991-2020%29_-_Atlantic_City_Area%2C_NJ%28ThreadEx%29.svg/250px-Monthly_Climate_Normals_%281991-2020%29_-_Atlantic_City_Area%2C_NJ%28ThreadEx%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Monthly_Climate_Normals_(1991-2020)_-_Atlantic_City_Area,_NJ(ThreadEx).svg)

Climate chart for Atlantic City

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") system, Atlantic City has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) with warm, moderately humid summers, cool winters and year-around precipitation. Cfa climates are characterized by all months having an average mean temperature above 32.0 °F (0.0 °C), at least four months with an average mean temperature at or above 50.0 °F (10.0 °C), at least one month with an average mean temperature at or above 72.0 °F (22.2 °C) and no significant precipitation difference between seasons. During the summer months in Atlantic City, a cooling afternoon [sea breeze](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze") is present on most days, but episodes of extreme heat and humidity can occur with [heat index](/wiki/Heat_index "Heat index") values at or above 95.0 °F (35.0 °C). During the winter months, episodes of extreme cold and wind can occur with [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill") values below 0.0 °F (−17.8 °C). The [plant hardiness zone](/wiki/Plant_hardiness_zone "Plant hardiness zone") at Atlantic City Beach is 7b with an average annual extreme minimum air temperature of 11.0 °F (−11.7 °C).[[99]](#cite_note-USDA-99) The average seasonal (November–April) snowfall total is 12 to 18 in (300 to 460 mm), and the average snowiest month is February which corresponds with the annual peak in [nor'easter](/wiki/Nor%27easter "Nor'easter") activity.

### Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ecology")]

According to the [A. W. Kuchler](/wiki/A._W._Kuchler "A. W. Kuchler") U.S. [potential natural vegetation](/wiki/Potential_natural_vegetation "Potential natural vegetation") types, Atlantic City would have a dominant vegetation type of Northern [Cordgrass](/wiki/Cordgrass "Cordgrass") (*73*) with a dominant vegetation form of [Coastal](/wiki/Coastal "Coastal") [Prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") (*20*).[[108]](#cite_note-Conservation_Biology_Institute-110)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 39,558 people, 15,504 households, and 8,558 families in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 3,680.8 per square mile (1,421.2/km2). There were 20,013 housing units at an average density of 1,862.2 per square mile (719.0/km2). The racial makeup was 26.65% (10,543) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 38.29% (15,148) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.61% (242) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 15.55% (6,153) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.05% (18) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 14.03% (5,549) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census#Race "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 4.82% (1,905) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 30.45% (12,044) of the population.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2010-29)

Of the 15,504 households, 27.3% had children under the age of 18; 25.9% were married couples living together; 22.2% had a female householder with no husband present and 44.8% were non-families. Of all households, 37.5% were made up of individuals and 14.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.50 and the average family size was 3.34.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2010-29)

People under the age of 18 account for 24.6%, 10.2% from 18 to 24, 26.8% from 25 to 44, 25.8% from 45 to 64, and 12.7% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 36.3 years. For every 100 females, the population had 96.2 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 94.4 males.[[29]](#cite_note-Census2010-29)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $30,237 (with a margin of error of +/− $2,354) and the median family income was $35,488 (+/− $2,607). Males had a median income of $32,207 (+/− $1,641) versus $29,298 (+/− $1,380) for females. The per capita income for the city was $20,069 (+/− $2,532). About 23.1% of families and 25.3% of the population were below the poverty line, including 36.6% of those under age 18 and 16.8% of those age 65 or over.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"),[[24]](#cite_note-GR2-24) there were 40,517 people, 15,848 households, and 8,700 families residing in the city. The population density was 3,569.8 inhabitants per square mile (1,378.3/km2). There were 20,219 housing units at an average density of 1,781.4 per square mile (687.8/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 44.16% black or African American, 26.68% White, 0.48% Native American, 10.40% Asian, 0.06% Pacific Islander, 13.76% other races, and 4.47% from two or more races. 24.95% of the population were Hispanic or Latino of any race. 19.44% of the population was non-Hispanic whites.[[117]](#cite_note-Census2000-119)[[118]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-120)

There were 15,848 households, out of which 27.7% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 24.8% were married couples living together, 23.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 45.1% were non-families. 37.2% of all households were made up of individuals, and 15.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.46 and the average family size was 3.26.[[117]](#cite_note-Census2000-119)[[118]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-120)

In the city the age distribution of the population shows 25.7% under the age of 18, 8.9% from 18 to 24, 31.0% from 25 to 44, 20.2% from 45 to 64, and 14.2% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 35 years. For every 100 females, there were 96.1 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 93.2 males.[[117]](#cite_note-Census2000-119)[[118]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-120)

The median income for a household in the city was $26,969, and the median income for a family was $31,997. Males had a median income of $25,471 versus $23,863 for females. The per capita income for the city was $15,402. About 19.1% of families and 23.6% of the population were below the poverty line, including 29.1% of those under age 18 and 18.9% of those age 65 or over.[[117]](#cite_note-Census2000-119)[[118]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-120)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

In September 2014, the greater Atlantic City area had one of the highest unemployment rates in the country at 13.8%, out of a labor force of around 141,000.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Tourism district

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tourism district")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Michigan_Avenue.jpeg/250px-Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Michigan_Avenue.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Michigan_Avenue.jpeg)

Atlantic City boardwalk at Michigan Avenue

In July 2010, Governor [Chris Christie](/wiki/Chris_Christie "Chris Christie") announced that a state takeover of the city and local government "was imminent". Comparing regulations in Atlantic City to an "antique car", Atlantic City regulatory reform was a key piece of Governor Chris Christie's plan to reinvigorate an industry mired in a four-year slump in revenue and hammered by fresh competition from casinos in the surrounding states of [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), and more recently, [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"). In January 2011, Chris Christie announced the creation of the Atlantic City Tourism District, a state-run district encompassing the boardwalk casinos, the marina casinos, the [Atlantic City Outlets](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Outlets "Atlantic City Outlets"), and [Bader Field](/wiki/Bader_Field "Bader Field").[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125) [Fairleigh Dickinson University](/wiki/Fairleigh_Dickinson_University "Fairleigh Dickinson University")'s [PublicMind](/wiki/Fairleigh_Dickinson_University#PublicMind "Fairleigh Dickinson University") poll surveyed New Jersey voters' attitudes on the takeover. A February 2011 survey showed that 43% opposed the measure while 29% favored direct state oversight.[[124]](#cite_note-politicker-126) The poll also found that even South Jersey voters expressed opposition to the plan; 40% reported they opposed the measure and 37% reported they were in favor of it.[[124]](#cite_note-politicker-126)

On April 29, 2011, the boundaries for the state-run tourism district were set. The district would include heavier police presence, as well as beautification projects and infrastructure improvements. The CRDA would oversee all functions of the district and make changes to attract new businesses and attractions. New construction has already resulted in cases of [eminent domain](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain") being used to seize properties for development.[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128)

The tourism district would comprise several key areas in the city: the Marina District, [Ducktown](/wiki/Ducktown,_Atlantic_City "Ducktown, Atlantic City"), Chelsea, South Inlet, [Bader Field](/wiki/Bader_Field "Bader Field"), and Gardner's Basin. Also included are 10 roadways that lead into the district, including several in the city's northern end, or North Beach. Gardner's Basin, which is home to the Atlantic City Aquarium, was initially left out of the tourism district, while a residential neighborhood in the Chelsea section was removed from the final boundaries, owing to complaints from the city. Also, the inclusion of Bader Field in the district was controversial and received much scrutiny from mayor Lorenzo Langford, who cast the lone "no" vote on the creation of the district citing its inclusion.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

### Casinos and gambling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Casinos and gambling")]

The history of gambling in Atlantic City traces back to [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") and the 1920s, with racketeer [Louis Kuehnle](/wiki/Louis_Kuehnle "Louis Kuehnle") running an underground hotel and casino. [Enoch "Nucky" Johnson](/wiki/Enoch_L._Johnson "Enoch L. Johnson") followed and furthered Atlantic City's rise through the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties") as a destination for drinking, gambling, and nightlife.[[128]](#cite_note-Great.com-130) In 1974, New Jersey voters voted 60%–40% against legalizing casino gambling at four sites statewide, but two years later approved by 56%–44% a new referendum which legalized casinos, but restricted them to Atlantic City.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133) [Resorts Atlantic City](/wiki/Resorts_Atlantic_City "Resorts Atlantic City") was the first casino to open, in May 1978, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony featuring [Governor of New Jersey](/wiki/Governor_of_New_Jersey "Governor of New Jersey") [Brendan Byrne](/wiki/Brendan_Byrne "Brendan Byrne").[[132]](#cite_note-134) Atlantic City is considered the "Gambling Capital of the East Coast", and currently has nine large casinos. In 2011, New Jersey's then 12 casinos employed approximately 33,000 employees, had 28.5 million visitors, made $3.3 billion in gaming revenue, and paid $278 million in taxes.[[133]](#cite_note-135) They are regulated by the [New Jersey Casino Control Commission](/wiki/New_Jersey_Casino_Control_Commission "New Jersey Casino Control Commission")[[134]](#cite_note-136) and the [New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement](/wiki/New_Jersey_Division_of_Gaming_Enforcement "New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement").[[135]](#cite_note-DGE-137)

In the wake of the economic downturn following the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and the legalization of gambling in adjacent and nearby states (including [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")), four casino closures took place in 2014: the Atlantic Club on January 13; the [Showboat](/wiki/Showboat_Atlantic_City "Showboat Atlantic City") on August 31;[[136]](#cite_note-138) the [Revel](/wiki/Revel_Atlantic_City "Revel Atlantic City"), which was Atlantic City's second-newest casino, on September 2;[[137]](#cite_note-139) and Trump Plaza, which originally opened in 1984, and was the poorest performing casino in the city, on September 16.[[138]](#cite_note-140)

Executives at Trump Entertainment Resorts, whose sole remaining property at the time was the Trump Taj Mahal, said in 2013 that they were considering the option of selling the Taj and winding down and exiting the gaming and hotel business.[[139]](#cite_note-141) Trump Taj Mahal closed October 10, 2016, after failing to come to terms with union workers.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

[Caesars Entertainment](/wiki/Caesars_Entertainment_Corporation "Caesars Entertainment Corporation") executives have been reconsidering the future of their three remaining Atlantic City properties (Bally's, Caesars and Harrah's), in the wake of a [Chapter 11 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_11_bankruptcy "Chapter 11 bankruptcy") filing by the company's casino operating unit in January 2015.[[141]](#cite_note-143) In 2020, Bally's Atlantic City was acquired by [Bally's Corporation](/wiki/Bally%27s_Corporation "Bally's Corporation").[[142]](#cite_note-reviewjournal.com-144) Gross gaming revenue of the city's nine operating casinos in 2022 totaled $2.79 billion, a 9% increase from the $2.55 billion earned the previous year.[[143]](#cite_note-145)

### Boardwalk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Boardwalk")]

Main article: [Atlantic City Boardwalk](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Boardwalk "Atlantic City Boardwalk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Boardwalk_in_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-Boardwalk_in_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boardwalk_in_Atlantic_City.jpg)

Boardwalk in Atlantic City

The Atlantic City Boardwalk opened on June 26, 1870,[[154]](#cite_note-June26-1870-156) a temporary structure erected for the summer season that was the first [boardwalk](/wiki/Boardwalk_(entertainment_district) "Boardwalk (entertainment district)") in the world.[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest1-159) At 5+1⁄2 miles (8.9 km) long, the Atlantic City Boardwalk is also the world's longest and busiest boardwalk.[[158]](#cite_note-AtlanticCityBoardwalkWorld'sLongestBusiestOldest2-160)

The Boardwalk starts at Absecon Inlet in the north and runs along the beach south-west to the city limit 4 mi (6.4 km) away then continues 1+1⁄2 mi (2.4 km) into [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey"). Casino/hotels front the boardwalk, as well as retail stores, restaurants, and amusements. Notable attractions include the [Boardwalk Hall](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall "Boardwalk Hall"), [House of Blues](/wiki/House_of_Blues "House of Blues"), and the [Ripley's Believe It or Not!](/wiki/Ripley%27s_Believe_It_or_Not! "Ripley's Believe It or Not!") museum.

In October 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") destroyed the northern part of the boardwalk fronting Absecon Inlet, in the residential section called South Inlet. The oceanfront boardwalk in front of the Atlantic City casinos survived the storm with minimal damage.[[159]](#cite_note-161)[[160]](#cite_note-162)

The first pier along the boardwalk, Applegate's Pier, opened in 1884.[[161]](#cite_note-163) It was acquired by John L. Young in 1891, who expanded and operated it as Young's Ocean Pier, but it was mostly destroyed in a 1912 fire. The remaining part of the pier was rebuilt in 1922 as the Central Pier, which is still in operation.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

A [Heinz](/wiki/Heinz "Heinz")-owned pier named Heinz Pier was destroyed in the [1944 Great Atlantic hurricane](/wiki/1944_Great_Atlantic_hurricane "1944 Great Atlantic hurricane").[[163]](#cite_note-165)

The most famous Atlantic City pier was [Steel Pier](/wiki/Steel_Pier "Steel Pier"), which opened in 1898, and which once billed itself as "The Showplace of the Nation". It closed in 1978, and was mostly destroyed in a 1982 fire. It was rebuilt in the late 1980s and is now operated as an amusement pier across from the [Hard Rock](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Atlantic_City "Hard Rock Hotel & Casino Atlantic City").

Steeplechase Pier opened in 1899 and operated until 1986. It suffered significant damage in a 1988 fire, and the remnants of the pier were removed in 1996. The "Steeplechase Pier Heliport" on Steel Pier is named in its honor.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

Captain John L. Young opened "Young's [Million Dollar Pier](/wiki/ACX1_Studios "ACX1 Studios")" in 1906, and on the seaward side "erected a marble mansion", fronted by a formal garden, with lighting and landscaping designed by Young's longtime friend [Thomas Alva Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Alva_Edison "Thomas Alva Edison"). Million Dollar Pier once rivaled Steel Pier as Atlantic City's leading pier,[[38]](#cite_note-Johnson,_Nelson_2010_30-38) but after suffering decades of decline, was rebuilt into a shopping mall in the 1980s, known as "Shops on Ocean One". In 2006, the Ocean One mall was bought, renovated and re-branded as "The Pier Shops at Caesars" and in 2015, it was renamed "[Playground Pier](/wiki/Playground_Pier "Playground Pier")". In September 2023, it was renamed "ACX1 Studios" for the film and entertainment production studios that took over the building, with a planned grand reopening containing a mixture of retail, restaurants, creative space, and production studios in summer of 2024.

[Garden Pier](/wiki/Garden_Pier "Garden Pier"), located opposite [Ocean Casino Resort](/wiki/Ocean_Casino_Resort "Ocean Casino Resort"), once housed a movie theater, and is now home to the Atlantic City Historical Museum.[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Panoramic_view_of_The_Pier_Shops_at_Caesars.jpg/960px-Panoramic_view_of_The_Pier_Shops_at_Caesars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panoramic_view_of_The_Pier_Shops_at_Caesars.jpg "Panoramic view of Playground Pier")

Panoramic view of [Playground Pier](/wiki/Playground_Pier "Playground Pier")

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Shopping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/The_Quarter_at_Tropicana_in_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-The_Quarter_at_Tropicana_in_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Quarter_at_Tropicana_in_Atlantic_City.jpg)

[The Quarter at Tropicana](/wiki/The_Quarter_at_Tropicana "The Quarter at Tropicana")

Atlantic City has many different shopping districts and malls, many of which are located inside or adjacent to the casino resorts. Several smaller themed retail and dining areas in casino hotels include the Borgata Shops and The Shoppes at Water Club inside Borgata, the Waterfront Shops inside of Harrah's, Spice Road inside the Trump Taj Mahal, while Resorts Casino Hotel has a small collection of stores and restaurants. Major shopping malls are also located in and around Atlantic City.

Atlantic City shops include:

* [Playground Pier](/wiki/Playground_Pier "Playground Pier"), an underwater-themed indoor high end shopping center located on the Million Dollar Pier formerly known as "Shops on Ocean One". The four-story shopping mall contains themed floors.
* [Tanger Outlets The Walk](/wiki/Tanger_Outlets_The_Walk "Tanger Outlets The Walk"), an outdoor outlet shopping center spanning several blocks. The only outlet mall in Atlantic County, The Walk opened in 2003 and is undergoing an expansion.
* [The Quarter at Tropicana](/wiki/The_Quarter_at_Tropicana "The Quarter at Tropicana"), an old [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana")-themed indoor shopping center at the Tropicana, which contains over 40 stores, restaurants, and nightclubs.

### Exhibition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Exhibition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Acconvention.jpg/250px-Acconvention.jpg)](/wiki/File:Acconvention.jpg)

[Atlantic City Convention Center](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Convention_Center "Atlantic City Convention Center")

[Boardwalk Hall](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall "Boardwalk Hall"), formally known as the "Historic Atlantic City Convention Hall", is an [arena](/wiki/Arena "Arena") in Atlantic City along the boardwalk. Boardwalk Hall was Atlantic City's primary [convention center](/wiki/Convention_center "Convention center") until the opening of the [Atlantic City Convention Center](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Convention_Center "Atlantic City Convention Center") in 1997. The Atlantic City Convention Center includes 500,000 sq ft (46,000 m2) of showroom space, 5 exhibit halls, 45 meeting rooms with 109,000 sq ft (10,100 m2) of space, a garage with 1,400 parking spaces, and an adjacent [Sheraton](/wiki/Sheraton_Hotels_and_Resorts "Sheraton Hotels and Resorts") hotel. Both the Boardwalk Hall and Convention Center are operated by the Atlantic City Convention & Visitors Authority.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Atlantic_City%E2%80%94_America%E2%80%99s_All-Year_Resort%2C_Pennsylvania_Railroad%2C_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City%E2%80%94_America%E2%80%99s_All-Year_Resort%2C_Pennsylvania_Railroad%2C_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City%E2%80%94_America%E2%80%99s_All-Year_Resort,_Pennsylvania_Railroad,_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg)

1930s Atlantic City by [Edward Mason Eggleston](/wiki/Edward_Mason_Eggleston "Edward Mason Eggleston")

### *Monopoly*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Monopoly")]

Atlantic City (sometimes referred to as "Monopoly City"[[1]](#cite_note-Monopoly-1)) has become well-known over the years for its portrayal in the U.S. version of the popular board game *[Monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly_(game) "Monopoly (game)")*, in which properties on the board are named after locations in and near Atlantic City. While the original incarnation of the game did not feature Atlantic City, it was in Indianapolis that Ruth Hoskins learned the game, and took it back to Atlantic City.[[167]](#cite_note-169) After she arrived, Hoskins made a new board with Atlantic City street names, and taught it to a group of friends, who ultimately passed in on to [Charles Darrow](/wiki/Charles_Darrow "Charles Darrow"), who made some modifications to the game and claimed it as his own invention.[[168]](#cite_note-170) The relative prices of the places on the board reflect to some extent the social status of neighborhoods at the time, with wealthy white streets being worth more, and streets where Black and Asian residents lived being cheaper.[[169]](#cite_note-171)

Marvin Gardens, the leading yellow property on the board, is actually a misspelling of the original location name, "[Marven Gardens](/wiki/Marven_Gardens "Marven Gardens")". The misspelling was said to have been introduced by Charles Todd and passed on when his home-made *Monopoly* board was copied by Charles Darrow and thence Parker Brothers. It was not until 1995 that Parker Brothers acknowledged this mistake and formally apologized to the residents of Marven Gardens for the misspelling, although the spelling error was not corrected.[[170]](#cite_note-172)

Some of the actual locations that correspond to board elements have changed since the game's release. Illinois Avenue was renamed [Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Boulevard_(Atlantic_City) "Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard (Atlantic City)") in the 1980s. St. Charles Place no longer exists, as the [Showboat Casino Hotel](/wiki/Showboat_Casino_Hotel "Showboat Casino Hotel") was developed where it once ran.[[171]](#cite_note-173)

The "Short Line" is believed to refer to the [Shore Fast Line](/wiki/Atlantic_City_and_Shore_Railroad "Atlantic City and Shore Railroad"), a [streetcar line](/wiki/Tram "Tram") that served Atlantic City,[[172]](#cite_note-174) or a bus route.[[173]](#cite_note-NYT1997-175) The [B&O Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") did not serve Atlantic City. A booklet included with the reprinted 1935 edition states that the four railroads that served Atlantic City in the mid-1930s were the [Jersey Central](/wiki/Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey "Central Railroad of New Jersey"), the [Seashore Lines](/wiki/Pennsylvania-Reading_Seashore_Lines "Pennsylvania-Reading Seashore Lines"), the [Reading Railroad](/wiki/Reading_Company "Reading Company"), and the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The actual "Electric Company" and "Water Works" serving the city are the [Atlantic City Electric Company](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Electric_Company "Atlantic City Electric Company") and the Atlantic City Municipal Utilities Authority, respectively.[[173]](#cite_note-NYT1997-175)

### Attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Attractions")]

Ever since Atlantic City's growth as a resort town, numerous attractions and [tourist traps](/wiki/Tourist_trap "Tourist trap") have originated in the city. A popular fixture in the early 20th century at the Steel Pier was [horse diving](/wiki/Horse_diving "Horse diving"), which was introduced by [William "Doc" Carver](/wiki/William_Frank_Carver "William Frank Carver").[[174]](#cite_note-Dedicated_to_The_Diving_Horses-176) The Steel Pier featured several other novelty attractions, including the Diving Bell, human high-divers and a water circus.[[175]](#cite_note-177)[[176]](#cite_note-178) Advertisements for the Steel Pier in its heyday featured plaster sculptures set upon wooden bases along roads leading up to Atlantic City.[[177]](#cite_note-179) By the end of World War II, many animal demonstrations declined in popularity after criticisms of animal abuse and neglect.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/A307%2C_Boardwalk%2C_Atlantic_City%2C_New_Jersey%2C_USA%2C_2012.JPG/250px-A307%2C_Boardwalk%2C_Atlantic_City%2C_New_Jersey%2C_USA%2C_2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:A307,_Boardwalk,_Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey,_USA,_2012.JPG)

Rolling chair on the boardwalk in winter 2012

Rolling chairs were introduced in 1876 for handicapped visitors and have been in continuous use since 1887 when they were made available to the general public, and have been a boardwalk fixture ever since.[[178]](#cite_note-180) While powered carts appeared in the 1960s, the original and most common were made of wicker. The wicker canopied chairs-on-wheels are manually pushed the length of the boardwalk by attendants, much like a [rickshaw](/wiki/Pulled_rickshaw "Pulled rickshaw").[[179]](#cite_note-181) Tramcars carrying 25 passengers each were introduced in June 1973, by which time the number of rolling carts had dropped from the thousands to as few as 10.[[180]](#cite_note-182) A tram service was introduced in 2015, initially using the warning announcements that had been used on [Wildwood's tramcars](/wiki/Tramcar_(Wildwood,_New_Jersey) "Tramcar (Wildwood, New Jersey)") until it was changed after complaints from [Wildwood](/wiki/Wildwood,_New_Jersey "Wildwood, New Jersey").[[181]](#cite_note-183)

The [Absecon Lighthouse](/wiki/Absecon_Lighthouse "Absecon Lighthouse") is a coastal [lighthouse](/wiki/Lighthouse "Lighthouse") located in the South Inlet section of Atlantic City overlooking [Absecon Inlet](/wiki/Absecon_Inlet "Absecon Inlet").[[182]](#cite_note-184) It is the tallest lighthouse in the state of New Jersey and is the third tallest masonry lighthouse in the United States. Construction began in 1854, with the light first lit on January 15, 1857.[[35]](#cite_note-LighthouseHistory-35) The lighthouse was deactivated in 1933 and although the light still shines every night, it is no longer an active navigational aid.[[183]](#cite_note-185) Gardner's Basin, which is home to the Atlantic City Aquarium as well as small shops and restaurants, is located a short distance north of Absecon Light.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Lucy_the_Elephant.jpg/250px-Lucy_the_Elephant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lucy_the_Elephant.jpg)

[Lucy the Elephant](/wiki/Lucy_the_Elephant "Lucy the Elephant") in nearby [Margate City](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey")

While located 2 mi (3.2 km) south of Atlantic City in [Margate City](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey"), [Lucy the Elephant](/wiki/Lucy_the_Elephant "Lucy the Elephant") has become almost an icon for the Atlantic City area. Lucy is a six-story elephant-shaped example of [novelty architecture](/wiki/Novelty_architecture "Novelty architecture"), constructed of wood and tin sheeting in 1882 by [James V. Lafferty](/wiki/James_V._Lafferty "James V. Lafferty") in an effort to sell real estate and attract tourism. Over the years, Lucy had served as a restaurant, business office, cottage, and tavern (the last closed by [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States")). Lucy had fallen into disrepair by the 1960s and was scheduled for demolition. The structure was moved and refurbished as a result of a "Save Lucy" campaign in 1970 and received designation as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1976, and is open as a museum.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

### Miss America pageant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Miss America pageant")]

Atlantic City was the home of the [Miss America](/wiki/Miss_America "Miss America") competition, hosting the event from its inception until 2004, and again from 2013 to 2018. The Miss America competition originated on September 7, 1921, as a two-day beauty contest, and it included state contestants as well as women from various cities around the country. The event that year was called the "Atlantic City Pageant", and the winner of the grand prize, Margaret Gorman, took home the 3-foot Golden Mermaid trophy. Gorman was not called "Miss America" until 1922, when she re-entered the pageant and lost to Mary Campbell.[[186]](#cite_note-188) The pageant was initiated to extend the tourist season after the [Labor Day weekend](/wiki/Labor_Day_(United_States) "Labor Day (United States)").[[53]](#cite_note-Good2010-53) The pageant has been nationally [televised](/wiki/Television "Television") since 1954. It peaked in the early 1960s, when it was repeatedly the highest-rated program on American television. It was seen as a symbol of the United States, with Miss America often being referred to as the female equivalent of the President. The pageant's longtime [emcee](/wiki/Master_of_ceremonies "Master of ceremonies"), [Bert Parks](/wiki/Bert_Parks "Bert Parks"), hosted the event from 1955 to 1979. At the [Atlantic City Convention Center](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Convention_Center "Atlantic City Convention Center"), there is a 400 lb (180 kg) interactive statue of Parks holding a crown. When a visitor puts their head inside the crown, sensors activate a recorded playback of his *"There She Is..."* line through speakers hidden behind nearby bushes.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Miss_America_Plaque%2C_Convention_Hall%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-Miss_America_Plaque%2C_Convention_Hall%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miss_America_Plaque,_Convention_Hall,_Atlantic_City.jpg)

Plaque for the Site of Previous Miss American Pageants

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Miss_America_Statue%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg/250px-Miss_America_Statue%2C_Atlantic_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miss_America_Statue,_Atlantic_City.jpg)
