### *Boardwalk Empire*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Boardwalk Empire")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Brighton_Avenue.jpeg/250px-Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Brighton_Avenue.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_boardwalk_looking_north_at_Brighton_Avenue.jpeg)

Atlantic City boardwalk at Brighton Avenue

The television show *[Boardwalk Empire](/wiki/Boardwalk_Empire "Boardwalk Empire")*, an American series from cable network [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") set in Atlantic City during the [Prohibition era](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), raised interest in the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties")-era city. Starring [Steve Buscemi](/wiki/Steve_Buscemi "Steve Buscemi"), the show was adapted from a chapter about historical criminal kingpin [Enoch "Nucky" Johnson](/wiki/Enoch_L._Johnson "Enoch L. Johnson") (who is renamed "Enoch Thompson" in the show) in Nelson Johnson's book, *Boardwalk Empire: The Birth, High Times, and Corruption of Atlantic City*.[[188]](#cite_note-190)[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192)[[191]](#cite_note-193)[[192]](#cite_note-194)[[193]](#cite_note-195)[[194]](#cite_note-196)

### Festivals and other recurring arts events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Festivals and other recurring arts events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Atlantic_City%E2%80%94America%27s_Great_All_Year_Resort%2C_Pennsylvania_Railroad%2C_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City%E2%80%94America%27s_Great_All_Year_Resort%2C_Pennsylvania_Railroad%2C_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City%E2%80%94America%27s_Great_All_Year_Resort,_Pennsylvania_Railroad,_painting_by_Edward_Mason_Eggleston.jpg)

1930s Atlantic City promotional art by [Edward Mason Eggleston](/wiki/Edward_Mason_Eggleston "Edward Mason Eggleston").

* The [TidalWave Music Festival](/wiki/TidalWave_Music_Festival "TidalWave Music Festival") is held on the Atlantic City beach in August, featuring [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"). Acts have included [Luke Bryan](/wiki/Luke_Bryan "Luke Bryan"), [Morgan Wallen](/wiki/Morgan_Wallen "Morgan Wallen"), [Dierks Bentley](/wiki/Dierks_Bentley "Dierks Bentley"), [Brooks & Dunn](/wiki/Brooks_%26_Dunn "Brooks & Dunn") and [Jason Aldean](/wiki/Jason_Aldean "Jason Aldean").[[195]](#cite_note-197)
* The [North to Shore Festival](/wiki/North_to_Shore_Festival "North to Shore Festival") debuts in June 2023, billed as New Jersey's answer to [SXSW](/wiki/SXSW "SXSW"). It will feature music and other entertainment at events in [Asbury Park](/wiki/Asbury_Park,_New_Jersey "Asbury Park, New Jersey"), Atlantic City and [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") throughout the month of June.[[196]](#cite_note-198)
* The Adjacent Music Festival will hold its inaugural event in May 2023, centered around emo, pop-punk, and hardcore music, with [Paramore](/wiki/Paramore "Paramore") and [Blink-182](/wiki/Blink-182 "Blink-182") on the Atlantic City beach.[[197]](#cite_note-199)
* The first Frantic City indie and punk festival took place in 2022 at Atlantic City's recently opened [Orange Loop Amphitheater](/wiki/The_Orange_Loop "The Orange Loop") with [Fred Armisen](/wiki/Fred_Armisen "Fred Armisen") as MC.[[198]](#cite_note-200)
* [The Bamboozle](/wiki/The_Bamboozle "The Bamboozle") festival was scheduled for [Bader Field](/wiki/Bader_Field "Bader Field") in May 2023, with acts including [E-Town Concrete](/wiki/E-Town_Concrete "E-Town Concrete"), [Coi Leray](/wiki/Coi_Leray "Coi Leray"), [Ice Spice](/wiki/Ice_Spice "Ice Spice"), [Trippie Redd](/wiki/Trippie_Redd "Trippie Redd"), [Limp Bizkit](/wiki/Limp_Bizkit "Limp Bizkit"), [A Boogie Wit da Hoodie](/wiki/A_Boogie_Wit_da_Hoodie "A Boogie Wit da Hoodie"), and [Papa Roach](/wiki/Papa_Roach "Papa Roach"). The festival had been held from 2003 to 2012 in [Asbury Park](/wiki/Asbury_Park,_New_Jersey "Asbury Park, New Jersey") and in [East Rutherford](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey") at the [Meadowlands Sports Complex](/wiki/Meadowlands_Sports_Complex "Meadowlands Sports Complex").[[199]](#cite_note-201) The 2023 festival was cancelled weeks before the event, as required forms and fees had not been submitted to the city.[[200]](#cite_note-202)
* An [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ") event known as the "[Miss'd America Pageant](/wiki/Miss%27d_America "Miss'd America")" is held annually. Originally started in 1994 as a fundraiser for local LGBT charities, the event features [drag queens](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen") on the runway in a similar manner to the Miss America pageant.[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)
* Since 2003, Atlantic City has hosted [Thunder over the Boardwalk](/wiki/Thunder_over_the_Boardwalk "Thunder over the Boardwalk"), an annual [airshow](/wiki/Airshow "Airshow") over the boardwalk. The yearly event, a joint venture between the [New Jersey Air National Guard](/wiki/New_Jersey_Air_National_Guard "New Jersey Air National Guard")'s [177th Fighter Wing](/wiki/177th_Fighter_Wing "177th Fighter Wing") along with several casinos, attracts over 750,000 visitors each year.[[203]](#cite_note-205)

### Religious events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Religious events")]

* A [Ratha Yatra](/wiki/Ratha_Yatra "Ratha Yatra") is held by the ISKCON of Central Jersey in collaboration with the local Hindu community, with thousands of attendees every year.
* Faith and Law Enforcement March, held by the Atlantic City Police Department with various churches, temples and other religious institutions in the area.

### The Orange Loop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: The Orange Loop")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/St_Nick_Tolentine_AC_NJ.JPG/250px-St_Nick_Tolentine_AC_NJ.JPG)](/wiki/File:St_Nick_Tolentine_AC_NJ.JPG)

[St. Nicholas of Tolentine Church](/wiki/St._Nicholas_of_Tolentine_Church "St. Nicholas of Tolentine Church") on [the Orange Loop](/wiki/The_Orange_Loop "The Orange Loop").

[The Orange Loop](/wiki/The_Orange_Loop "The Orange Loop") is a neighborhood near the beach in Atlantic City with a focus on live music establishments like [Anchor Rock Club](/wiki/List_of_music_venues#New_Jersey "List of music venues") and Tennessee Beer Hall.[[204]](#cite_note-206)
It runs perpendicular from the boardwalk inland roughly to [St. Nicholas of Tolentine Church](/wiki/St._Nicholas_of_Tolentine_Church "St. Nicholas of Tolentine Church").[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208)
It is bounded by Tennessee Avenue, St. James Place, Pacific Avenue and the boardwalk, and derives its name from the orange color of those streets on a traditional [Monopoly gameboard](/wiki/Monopoly_(game) "Monopoly (game)").[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210)[[209]](#cite_note-211) The Orange Loop Amphitheater hosted the Frantic City [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") music festival on the loop in 2022.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

The [Atlantic City Race Course](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Race_Course "Atlantic City Race Course") in [Hamilton Township](/wiki/Hamilton_Township,_Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "Hamilton Township, Atlantic County, New Jersey") was a horse racing track that operated from 1946 to 2015.

The [ShopRite LPGA Classic](/wiki/ShopRite_LPGA_Classic "ShopRite LPGA Classic") is an [LPGA Tour](/wiki/LPGA_Tour "LPGA Tour") women's golf tournament held near Atlantic City since it started in 1986.[[218]](#cite_note-220)

### Professional boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Professional boxing")]

Since February 2, 1887, the city of Atlantic City has seen 2,538 (as of September 2018) professional boxing fight programs,[[219]](#cite_note-221) the first one being one with a main event fight between Willie Clark, 3-0-3, and debuting Horace Leeds, won by Clark on points over four rounds.[[220]](#cite_note-222) During the 1980s, professional boxing activity boomed in Atlantic City, at times rivaling [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"), in staging major boxing fights. Fighters who fought in Atlantic City at that era include [Marvelous Marvin Hagler](/wiki/Marvelous_Marvin_Hagler "Marvelous Marvin Hagler"), [Thomas Hearns](/wiki/Thomas_Hearns "Thomas Hearns"), [Wilfredo Gómez](/wiki/Wilfredo_G%C3%B3mez "Wilfredo Gómez"), [Jeff Chandler](/wiki/Jeff_Chandler_(boxer) "Jeff Chandler (boxer)"), [Larry Holmes](/wiki/Larry_Holmes "Larry Holmes"), [George Foreman](/wiki/George_Foreman "George Foreman"), [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson") and others. Fights included [The Brawl For it All](/wiki/The_Brawl_For_it_All "The Brawl For it All"), Tyson versus Holmes, Tyson versus [Michael Spinks](/wiki/Michael_Spinks "Michael Spinks"), and [Roberto Durán](/wiki/Roberto_Dur%C3%A1n "Roberto Durán") versus [Iran Barkley](/wiki/Iran_Barkley "Iran Barkley"). Many boxing matches were held at [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s [Trump Plaza](/wiki/Trump_Plaza_Hotel_and_Casino "Trump Plaza Hotel and Casino"), promoted either by [Bob Arum](/wiki/Bob_Arum "Bob Arum") or [Don King](/wiki/Don_King_(boxing_promoter) "Don King (boxing promoter)").

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Atlantic City is one of five municipalities in the state—and the only one outside of [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey")—that offer free public access to oceanfront beaches monitored by lifeguards, joining [Wildwood](/wiki/Wildwood,_New_Jersey "Wildwood, New Jersey"), [North Wildwood](/wiki/North_Wildwood,_New_Jersey "North Wildwood, New Jersey"), [Wildwood Crest](/wiki/Wildwood_Crest,_New_Jersey "Wildwood Crest, New Jersey") and [Upper Township](/wiki/Upper_Township,_New_Jersey "Upper Township, New Jersey")'s [Strathmere](/wiki/Strathmere,_New_Jersey "Strathmere, New Jersey") section.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

Further information: [Mayors of Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Mayors_of_Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Mayors of Atlantic City, New Jersey")

Atlantic City is governed within the [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act") (formally known as the Optional Municipal Charter Law) under the [Mayor-Council](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(mayor%E2%80%93council) "Faulkner Act (mayor–council)") system of municipal government (Plan D), implemented by direct petition effective as of July 1, 1982.[[5]](#cite_note-DataBook-5)[[222]](#cite_note-225)[[223]](#cite_note-226) The city is one of 71 municipalities (of the 564) statewide governed under this form.[[224]](#cite_note-227) The governing body of Atlantic City comprises the Mayor and the City Council, all elected on a partisan basis to serve four-year terms of office as part of the November general election. The council includes nine members, who are elected on a staggered basis, with one member from each of six [wards](/wiki/Ward_(United_States) "Ward (United States)") and three serving [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large"). The six ward seats are up for election together and the mayoral seat and the council at-large seats are up for vote together two years later. The City Council exercises the legislative power of the municipality for the purpose of holding Council meetings to introduce ordinances and resolutions to regulate City government. In addition, Council members review budgets submitted by the Mayor; provide for an annual audit of the city's accounts and financial transactions; organize standing committees and hold public hearings to address important issues which impact Atlantic City.[[225]](#cite_note-228) Former Mayor Bob Levy created the Atlantic City Ethics Board in 2007, but the Board was dissolved two years later by vote of the Atlantic City Council.

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the [Mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "List of mayors of Atlantic City, New Jersey") is Democrat [Marty Small Sr.](/wiki/Marty_Small_Sr. "Marty Small Sr."), whose term of office ends December 31, 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-Mayor-6) Small succeeded [Frank M. Gilliam Jr.](/wiki/Frank_Gilliam_(politician) "Frank Gilliam (politician)") following his resignation on October 3, 2019.[[226]](#cite_note-229) Small initially served as mayor on an interim basis for an unexpired term ending on December 31, 2021.[[227]](#cite_note-230) Members of the City Council are Council President Council President Aaron "Sporty" Randolph (D, 2027; 1st Ward), Council Vice President Kaleem Shabazz (D, 2027; 3rd Ward), George "Animal" Crouch (D, 2027; 4th Ward), LaToya Dunston (D, 2027; Second Ward – elected to serve an unexpired term), Jesse O. Kurtz ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), 2027; 6th Ward), Stephanie Marshall (D, 2025; At-Large), George Tibbitt (D, 2025; At-Large), Bruce Weekes (D, 2025; At-Large) and Maria Lacca (R, 2027; 5th Ward).[[8]](#cite_note-CityCouncil-8)[[228]](#cite_note-231)[[229]](#cite_note-Govt-232)[[230]](#cite_note-Atlantic2023-233)[[231]](#cite_note-Atlantic2021-234)

In 2024, Democrat Muhammed "Anjum" Zia, who served as a councilman for the 5th ward, was removed from office after it was found the he did not reside in Atlantic City, but instead with his family in Egg Harbor Township. He was ousted from the city council, and in a court-ordered special election that November, Republican Maria Lacca, who brought about the investigation against Zia, flipped the seat, becoming the first Republican to win it since 1988. She became one out of two Republicans now currently on Atlantic City's council, with the other being councilman Jesse O. Kurtz of the 6th ward.[[232]](#cite_note-235)[[233]](#cite_note-236)

In May 2020, voters rejected by a 3–1 margin a referendum that would have changed the city to a [council-manager](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(council%E2%80%93manager) "Faulkner Act (council–manager)") form of government which would have reduced the size of the city council and shifted responsibility for day-to-day operation from an elected mayor to an appointed [city manager](/wiki/City_manager "City manager").[[234]](#cite_note-237)

In December 2019, LaToya Dunston was selected from a list of three candidates nominated by the Democratic municipal committee to serve the remainder of the term of the Second Ward seat that had been held by Marty Small until he stepped down when he was appointed as mayor.[[235]](#cite_note-238) In January 2020, Dunston was appointed to fill the Second Ward seat expiring in December 2023 that Small had won in November 2019 but declined to fill; Dunston will serve on an interim basis until the November 2020 general election, when voters will select a candidate to serve the balance of the term of office.[[236]](#cite_note-239)

#### Mayoral disappearance and resignation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Mayoral disappearance and resignation")]

Following questions about false claims he had made about his military record, Mayor [Bob Levy](/wiki/Bob_Levy_(New_Jersey_politician)#False_claims_of_service_and_the_federal_investigation "Bob Levy (New Jersey politician)") left City Hall in September 2007 in a city-owned vehicle for an unknown destination. After a 13-day absence, his lawyer revealed that Levy was in [Carrier Clinic](/wiki/Carrier_Clinic "Carrier Clinic"), a rehabilitation hospital.[[237]](#cite_note-240) Levy resigned in October 2007 and then-Council President William Marsh assumed the office of Mayor[[238]](#cite_note-241) and served six weeks until an interim mayor was named.

### Federal, state and county representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Federal, state and county representation")]

Atlantic City is located in the 2nd Congressional district[[239]](#cite_note-PCR2012-242) and is part of New Jersey's 2nd state legislative district.[[240]](#cite_note-Districts2011-243)[[241]](#cite_note-LWV2019-244)[[242]](#cite_note-245)

For the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), [New Jersey's 2nd congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_congressional_district "New Jersey's 2nd congressional district") is represented by [Jeff Van Drew](/wiki/Jeff_Van_Drew "Jeff Van Drew") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Dennis Township](/wiki/Dennis_Township,_New_Jersey "Dennis Township, New Jersey")).[[243]](#cite_note-246) New Jersey is represented in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") ([Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), term ends 2027) and [Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim "Andy Kim") ([Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey"), term ends 2031).[[244]](#cite_note-247)

For the [2026–2027 session](/wiki/222nd_New_Jersey_Legislature "222nd New Jersey Legislature"), the [2nd legislative district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_legislative_district "New Jersey's 2nd legislative district") of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") is represented in the [New Jersey Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") by [Vincent J. Polistina](/wiki/Vincent_J._Polistina "Vincent J. Polistina") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey")) and in the [General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") by [Don Guardian](/wiki/Don_Guardian "Don Guardian") (R, Atlantic City) and [Maureen Rowan](/wiki/Maureen_Rowan "Maureen Rowan") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), Atlantic City).[[245]](#cite_note-248)

Atlantic County is governed by a directly elected [county executive](/wiki/County_executives_of_Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "County executives of Atlantic County, New Jersey") and a nine-member [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners "Board of County Commissioners"), responsible for legislation. The executive serves a four-year term and the commissioners are elected to staggered three-year terms, of which four are elected from the county on an [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") basis and five of the commissioners represent equally populated districts.[[246]](#cite_note-Freeholders-249)[[247]](#cite_note-250) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit), Atlantic County's Executive is Dennis Levinson ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Northfield](/wiki/Northfield,_New_Jersey "Northfield, New Jersey")), whose term of office ends December 31, 2027.[[248]](#cite_note-251) Members of the Board of County Commissioners are:

Ernest D. Coursey (D, District 1-- Atlantic City, [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey") (part), [Longport](/wiki/Longport,_New_Jersey "Longport, New Jersey"), [Margate City](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey"), and [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey"); 2025, Atlantic City),[[249]](#cite_note-252) Chair Maureen Kern (R, District 2-- [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey") (part), [Linwood](/wiki/Linwood,_New_Jersey "Linwood, New Jersey"), [Northfield](/wiki/Northfield,_New_Jersey "Northfield, New Jersey"), [Somers Point](/wiki/Somers_Point,_New_Jersey "Somers Point, New Jersey") and [Pleasantville](/wiki/Pleasantville,_New_Jersey "Pleasantville, New Jersey"); 2027, Somers Point),[[250]](#cite_note-253) Andrew Parker III (R, District 3-- [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey") (part) and [Hamilton Township](/wiki/Hamilton_Township,_Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "Hamilton Township, Atlantic County, New Jersey") (part); 2026, Egg Harbor Township),[[251]](#cite_note-254) Richard R. ### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Politics")]

As of March 23, 2011, there were a total of 20,001 registered voters in Atlantic City, of which 12,063 (60.3% vs. 30.5% countywide) were registered as [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), 1,542 (7.7% vs. 25.2%) were registered as [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and 6,392 (32.0% vs. 44.3%) were registered as [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Unaffiliated_(New_Jersey) "Unaffiliated (New Jersey)"). There were 4 voters registered to other parties.[[266]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-269) Among the city's 2010 Census population, 50.6% (vs. 58.8% in Atlantic County) were registered to vote, including 67.0% of those ages 18 and over (vs. 76.6% countywide).[[266]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-269)[[267]](#cite_note-270)

### City and state agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: City and state agencies")]

#### New Jersey Casino Control Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: New Jersey Casino Control Commission")]

Main article: [New Jersey Casino Control Commission](/wiki/New_Jersey_Casino_Control_Commission "New Jersey Casino Control Commission")

The New Jersey Casino Control Commission is a New Jersey state governmental agency that was founded in 1977 as the state's [Gaming Control Board](/wiki/Gaming_Control_Board "Gaming Control Board"), responsible for administering the Casino Control Act and its regulations to assure public trust and confidence in the credibility and integrity of the casino industry and casino operations in Atlantic City. Casinos operate under licenses granted by the commission. The commission is headquartered in the Arcade Building at Tennessee Avenue and Boardwalk in Atlantic City.[[282]](#cite_note-285)

#### New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement")]

Main article: [New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement](/wiki/New_Jersey_Division_of_Gaming_Enforcement "New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement")

The [New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement](/wiki/New_Jersey_Division_of_Gaming_Enforcement "New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement") is a division of the [New Jersey Department of Law and Public Safety](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Law_and_Public_Safety "New Jersey Department of Law and Public Safety") and is responsible for certifying casino gaming revenue, registering casino employees and non-gaming vendors, licensing gaming vendors, and handling all casino patron complaints.[[135]](#cite_note-DGE-137)

#### Casino Reinvestment Development Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Casino Reinvestment Development Authority")]

Main article: [Casino Reinvestment Development Authority](/wiki/Casino_Reinvestment_Development_Authority "Casino Reinvestment Development Authority")

The CRDA was founded in 1984 and is responsible for directing the spending of casino reinvestment funds in public and private projects to benefit Atlantic City and other areas of the state. From 1985 through April 2008, CRDA spent US$1.5 billion on projects in Atlantic City and US$300 million throughout New Jersey.[[283]](#cite_note-286)

#### Atlantic City Convention and Visitors Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Atlantic City Convention and Visitors Authority")]

The Convention & Visitors Authority (ACCVA) was in charge of advertising and marketing for the city as well as promoting economic growth through convention and leisure tourism development. The ACCVA managed the [Boardwalk Hall](/wiki/Boardwalk_Hall "Boardwalk Hall") and [Atlantic City Convention Center](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Convention_Center "Atlantic City Convention Center"), as well as the Boardwalk Welcome Center inside Boardwalk Hall and a welcome center on the [Atlantic City Expressway](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Expressway "Atlantic City Expressway"). In 2011, the ACCVA was absorbed into the CRDA as part of the state takeover that created the tourism district.[[284]](#cite_note-287)

#### Atlantic City Special Improvement District

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Atlantic City Special Improvement District")]

The Atlantic City Special Improvement District (SID) was a nonprofit organization created in 1992, funded by a special assessment tax on businesses within the improvement district. It carried out various activities to improve the city's business community, including street cleaning and promotional efforts. In 2011, the SID was absorbed by the CRDA; the former SID boundaries would be expanded to the include all areas in the newly formed tourism district. Under the new structure, established by state legislation, the CRDA assumed responsibility for the staff, equipment and programs of the SID. The new SID division includes a SID committee made up of CRDA board members and an advisory council consisting of the current trustees and others.[[285]](#cite_note-288)

The **Atlantic City Fire Department** (**ACFD**) provides [fire protection](/wiki/Fire_protection "Fire protection") and first responder [emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services") to the city. The ACFD operates out of six fire stations, located throughout the city in one battalion, under the command of a [battalion chief](/wiki/Battalion_chief "Battalion chief"), who in-turn reports to an on-duty Deputy Chief, or Tour Commander each shift.[[286]](#cite_note-ACFDHistory-289)[[288]](#cite_note-291)[[289]](#cite_note-292)

## Police department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Police department")]

Main article: [Atlantic City Police Department](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Police_Department "Atlantic City Police Department")

The city is protected by the [Atlantic City Police Department](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Police_Department "Atlantic City Police Department"), which handles 150,000 calls per year. The Chief of Police is James A. Sarkos.[[290]](#cite_note-293)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Atlantic City School District](/wiki/Atlantic_City_School_District "Atlantic City School District") serves students in [pre-kindergarten](/wiki/Pre-kindergarten "Pre-kindergarten") through [twelfth grades](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade").[[291]](#cite_note-294) As of the 2020–21 school year, the district, comprised of 11 schools, had an enrollment of 6,553 students and 617.3 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 10.6:1.[[292]](#cite_note-NCES-295) Schools in the district (with 2020–21 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[293]](#cite_note-296)) are
Venice Park School[[294]](#cite_note-297) (36 students in PreK),
Brighton Avenue School[[295]](#cite_note-298) (315 students; in grades PreK-5),
Chelsea Heights School[[296]](#cite_note-299) (332; PreK-8),
Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. School Complex[[297]](#cite_note-300) (543; PreK-8),
New York Avenue School[[298]](#cite_note-301) (548; PreK-8),
Pennsylvania Avenue School[[299]](#cite_note-302) (549; PreK-8),
Richmond Avenue School[[300]](#cite_note-303) (615; PreK-8),
Sovereign Avenue School[[301]](#cite_note-304) (696; PreK-8),
Texas Avenue School[[302]](#cite_note-305) (499; K-8),
Uptown School Complex[[303]](#cite_note-306) (536; PreK-8) and
[Atlantic City High School](/wiki/Atlantic_City_High_School "Atlantic City High School")[[304]](#cite_note-307) (1,771; 9–12).[[305]](#cite_note-Schools-308)[[306]](#cite_note-309)[[307]](#cite_note-310) Pennsylvania Avenue School opened for the 2012–13 school year, with most students shifting from New Jersey Avenue School, which had been one of the district's oldest and most rundown schools.[[308]](#cite_note-311)

Students from [Brigantine](/wiki/Brigantine,_New_Jersey "Brigantine, New Jersey"), [Longport](/wiki/Longport,_New_Jersey "Longport, New Jersey"), [Margate City](/wiki/Margate_City,_New_Jersey "Margate City, New Jersey") and [Ventnor City](/wiki/Ventnor_City,_New_Jersey "Ventnor City, New Jersey") attend Atlantic City High School as part of [sending/receiving relationships](/wiki/Sending/receiving_relationship "Sending/receiving relationship") with the respective school districts.[[309]](#cite_note-312)[[310]](#cite_note-313)

City public school students are also eligible to attend the [Atlantic County Institute of Technology](/wiki/Atlantic_County_Institute_of_Technology "Atlantic County Institute of Technology") in the [Mays Landing](/wiki/Mays_Landing,_New_Jersey "Mays Landing, New Jersey") section of [Hamilton Township](/wiki/Hamilton_Township,_Atlantic_County,_New_Jersey "Hamilton Township, Atlantic County, New Jersey")[[311]](#cite_note-314) or the [Charter-Tech High School for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Charter-Tech_High_School_for_the_Performing_Arts "Charter-Tech High School for the Performing Arts"), located in [Somers Point](/wiki/Somers_Point,_New_Jersey "Somers Point, New Jersey").[[312]](#cite_note-315)

Oceanside Charter School, which offered pre-Kindergarten through [eighth grade](/wiki/Eighth_grade "Eighth grade"), was founded in 1999 and closed in June 2013 when its charter was not renewed by the [New Jersey Department of Education](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Education "New Jersey Department of Education").[[313]](#cite_note-316)

Founded in 1908, Our Lady Star of the Sea Regional School is a [Catholic elementary school](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school"), operates under the jurisdiction of the [Diocese of Camden](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Camden "Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden").[[314]](#cite_note-317)[[315]](#cite_note-318)

Nearby college campuses include those of [Atlantic Cape Community College](/wiki/Atlantic_Cape_Community_College "Atlantic Cape Community College") and [Stockton University](/wiki/Stockton_University "Stockton University"), the latter of which offers classes and resources in the city such as the [Carnegie Library Center](/wiki/Carnegie_Library_Center "Carnegie Library Center"), which was turned back to the control of the city in April 2022.[[316]](#cite_note-319)

Eastern terminus of the [Atlantic City Expressway](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Expressway "Atlantic City Expressway") in Atlantic City

#### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the city had a total of 103.67 mi (166.84 km) of roadways, of which 88.26 mi (142.04 km) were maintained by the municipality, 1.29 mi (2.08 km) by Atlantic County, 5.32 mi (8.56 km) by the [New Jersey Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Transportation "New Jersey Department of Transportation") and 8.80 mi (14.16 km) by the [South Jersey Transportation Authority](/wiki/South_Jersey_Transportation_Authority "South Jersey Transportation Authority").[[317]](#cite_note-320)

The three roadways that enter Atlantic City are: [Black Horse Pike](/wiki/Black_Horse_Pike "Black Horse Pike")/Harding Highway ([US 322](/wiki/U.S._Route_322_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 322 in New Jersey")/[40](/wiki/U.S._Route_40_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 40 in New Jersey") via the Albany Avenue [drawbridge](/wiki/Drawbridge "Drawbridge")), White Horse Pike ([US 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 30 in New Jersey")), and the [Atlantic City Expressway](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Expressway "Atlantic City Expressway") through the [Brigantine Connector](/wiki/Atlantic_City%E2%80%93Brigantine_Connector "Atlantic City–Brigantine Connector"). Atlantic City is roughly 132 mi (212 km) south of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") via the [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway") and 55 mi (89 km) southeast of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[318]](#cite_note-321)

#### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Interior_of_Atlantic_City_Rail_Terminal%2C_August_2014.jpg/250px-Interior_of_Atlantic_City_Rail_Terminal%2C_August_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_Atlantic_City_Rail_Terminal,_August_2014.jpg)

[Atlantic City Rail Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Rail_Terminal "Atlantic City Rail Terminal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Atlantic_City_Jitney_Association_Champion_29.jpg/250px-Atlantic_City_Jitney_Association_Champion_29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_City_Jitney_Association_Champion_29.jpg)

[ACJA](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Jitney_Association "Atlantic City Jitney Association") "Jitney" No. 29 on a casino shuttle run

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/NJ_Transit_Nova_RTS_2514.jpg/250px-NJ_Transit_Nova_RTS_2514.jpg)](/wiki/File:NJ_Transit_Nova_RTS_2514.jpg)

[NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit_Bus_Operations "NJ Transit Bus Operations") #2514 on the [505](/wiki/List_of_NJ_Transit_bus_routes_(500%E2%80%93549) "List of NJ Transit bus routes (500–549)")

Atlantic City is connected to other cities in several ways. [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit")'s [Atlantic City Rail Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Rail_Terminal "Atlantic City Rail Terminal")[[319]](#cite_note-322) at the [Atlantic City Convention Center](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Convention_Center "Atlantic City Convention Center") provides service from [30th Street Station](/wiki/30th_Street_Station "30th Street Station") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") through several smaller South Jersey communities via the [Atlantic City Line](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Line "Atlantic City Line").[[320]](#cite_note-323)

In June 2006, the board of NJ Transit approved a three-year trial of express train service between [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station") and the Atlantic City Rail Terminal. The line, known as ACES ([Atlantic City Express Service](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Express_Service "Atlantic City Express Service")), ran from February 2009 to March 2012. The approximate travel time was 2+1⁄2 hours, with a stop at [Newark Penn Station](/wiki/Newark_Penn_Station "Newark Penn Station"), and was part of the casinos' multimillion-dollar investments in Atlantic City. Most of the funding for the transit line was provided by [Harrah's Entertainment](/wiki/Harrah%27s_Entertainment "Harrah's Entertainment") (owners of both [Harrah's Atlantic City](/wiki/Harrah%27s_Atlantic_City "Harrah's Atlantic City") and [Caesars Atlantic City](/wiki/Caesars_Atlantic_City "Caesars Atlantic City")) and the [Borgata](/wiki/Borgata "Borgata"), which pulled the plug after millions in losses.[[321]](#cite_note-324)[[322]](#cite_note-325)

The [Atlantic City Bus Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Bus_Terminal "Atlantic City Bus Terminal") is the home to local, intrastate and interstate bus companies including NJ Transit, [OurBus](/wiki/OurBus "OurBus")[[323]](#cite_note-326) and [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") bus lines. The Greyhound Lucky Streak Express offers service to Atlantic City from New York City, [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), Philadelphia, [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[324]](#cite_note-327) In addition to stopping at the Atlantic City Bus Terminal, Greyhound buses stop at various casinos in Atlantic City. [Martz Trailways](/wiki/Martz_Group "Martz Group") provides bus service to various casinos in Atlantic City from [Wilkes-Barre](/wiki/Wilkes-Barre,_Pennsylvania "Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania"), [Scranton](/wiki/Scranton,_Pennsylvania "Scranton, Pennsylvania"), and [White Haven](/wiki/White_Haven,_Pennsylvania "White Haven, Pennsylvania") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[325]](#cite_note-328) [Klein Transportation](/wiki/Klein_Transportation "Klein Transportation") provides bus service to various casinos in Atlantic City from [Shillington](/wiki/Shillington,_Pennsylvania "Shillington, Pennsylvania"), [Douglassville](/wiki/Douglassville,_Pennsylvania "Douglassville, Pennsylvania"), [Royersford](/wiki/Royersford,_Pennsylvania "Royersford, Pennsylvania"), and [Audubon](/wiki/Audubon,_Pennsylvania "Audubon, Pennsylvania") in Pennsylvania.[[326]](#cite_note-329)

Within the city, public transportation is provided by [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit_Bus_Operations "NJ Transit Bus Operations") along 13 routes, including service between the city and the [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") on the [319](/wiki/319_(New_Jersey_bus) "319 (New Jersey bus)") route, and service to and from Atlantic City on routes [501](/wiki/501_(New_Jersey_bus) "501 (New Jersey bus)") (to [Brigantine Beach](/wiki/Brigantine_Beach,_New_Jersey "Brigantine Beach, New Jersey")), [502](/wiki/502_(New_Jersey_bus) "502 (New Jersey bus)") (to [Atlantic Cape Community College](/wiki/Atlantic_Cape_Community_College "Atlantic Cape Community College")), [504](/wiki/504_(New_Jersey_bus) "504 (New Jersey bus)") (to Ventnor Plaza), [505](/wiki/505_(New_Jersey_bus) "505 (New Jersey bus)") (to [Longport](/wiki/Longport,_New_Jersey "Longport, New Jersey")), [507](/wiki/507_(New_Jersey_bus) "507 (New Jersey bus)") (to [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_New_Jersey "Ocean City, New Jersey")), [508](/wiki/508_(New_Jersey_bus) "508 (New Jersey bus)") (to the [Hamilton Mall](/wiki/Hamilton_Mall "Hamilton Mall")), [509](/wiki/509_(New_Jersey_bus) "509 (New Jersey bus)") (to Ocean City), [551](/wiki/551_(New_Jersey_bus) "551 (New Jersey bus)") (to Philadelphia), [552](/wiki/552_(New_Jersey_bus) "552 (New Jersey bus)") (to [Cape May](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey")), [553](/wiki/553_(New_Jersey_bus) "553 (New Jersey bus)") The [Atlantic City Jitney Association](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Jitney_Association "Atlantic City Jitney Association") (ACJA) offers service on four fixed-route lines and on shuttles to and from the rail terminal.[[329]](#cite_note-332)

#### Airline service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Airline service")]

Commercial airlines serving Atlantic City include [Atlantic City International Airport](/wiki/Atlantic_City_International_Airport "Atlantic City International Airport"), located 9 mi (14 km) northwest of the city in [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township,_New_Jersey "Egg Harbor Township, New Jersey"). Many travelers also fly into [Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport"), [Trenton-Mercer Airport](/wiki/Trenton-Mercer_Airport "Trenton-Mercer Airport"), or [Newark Liberty International Airport](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport "Newark Liberty International Airport"), where there are wider selections of carriers from which to choose. The historic downtown [Bader Field](/wiki/Bader_Field "Bader Field") airport is now permanently closed and plans are in the works to redevelop the land. In 2022, the *CASA MAR* project was announced, a $3 billion plan to redevelop Bader Field into a mixed-use complex that includes housing and employment opportunities.[[330]](#cite_note-333)

Atlantic City International Airport, which is served by various scheduled [chartered flight](/wiki/Chartered_flight "Chartered flight") company, is a focus city for [Spirit Airlines](/wiki/Spirit_Airlines "Spirit Airlines")

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/AtlantiCare_Regional_Medical_Center_Atlantic_City%2C_NJ.jpg/250px-AtlantiCare_Regional_Medical_Center_Atlantic_City%2C_NJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:AtlantiCare_Regional_Medical_Center_Atlantic_City,_NJ.jpg)

[AtlantiCare Regional Medical Center, Mainland Campus](/wiki/AtlantiCare_Regional_Medical_Center,_Mainland_Campus "AtlantiCare Regional Medical Center, Mainland Campus") in [Galloway Township](/wiki/Galloway_Township,_New_Jersey "Galloway Township, New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Atlantic-Jersey_Wind_Farm.jpg/250px-Atlantic-Jersey_Wind_Farm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic-Jersey_Wind_Farm.jpg)

[Jersey-Atlantic Wind Farm](/wiki/Jersey-Atlantic_Wind_Farm "Jersey-Atlantic Wind Farm") is the first coastal wind farm in the United States.[[331]](#cite_note-334)

The [AtlantiCare Regional Medical Center](/wiki/AtlantiCare_Regional_Medical_Center_City_Division "AtlantiCare Regional Medical Center City Division") is a health system based in Atlantic City. Founded in 1898, it includes two hospitals; the Atlantic City Campus and the Mainland Campus in [Pomona, New Jersey](/wiki/Pomona,_New_Jersey "Pomona, New Jersey"). It has Atlantic City's only cancer institute, heart institute, and neonatal intensive care unit.[[332]](#cite_note-335)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[South Jersey Industries](/wiki/South_Jersey_Industries "South Jersey Industries") provides natural gas to the city under the South Jersey Gas division. Marina Energy and its subsidiary, Energenic, a [joint business venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") with a long-time [business partner](/wiki/Business_partner "Business partner"), operate two [thermal power stations](/wiki/Thermal_power_station "Thermal power station") in the city. The Marina Thermal Plant serves the Borgata while a second plant serves the Resorts Hotel and Casino.[[333]](#cite_note-336) Another thermal plant is the Midtown Thermal Control Center on Atlantic and Ohio Avenues built by Conectiv, which opened in 1997 and provides chilled water for hotels and other facilities along the Boardwalk.[[334]](#cite_note-337)

Electrical power in Atlantic City and its immediate surrounding area is provided primarily served [Atlantic City Electric](/wiki/Pepco_Holdings "Pepco Holdings"), which was incorporated in 1924 and provides power from the [Beesley's Point Generating Station](/wiki/Beesley%27s_Point_Generating_Station "Beesley's Point Generating Station") in [Upper Township](/wiki/Upper_Township,_New_Jersey "Upper Township, New Jersey") and other locations.[[335]](#cite_note-338)

The [Jersey-Atlantic Wind Farm](/wiki/Jersey-Atlantic_Wind_Farm "Jersey-Atlantic Wind Farm"), opened in 2005, is the first onshore coastal [wind farm](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") in the United States.[[336]](#cite_note-339) In October 2010, North American Offshore Wind Conference was held in the city and included tours of the facility and potential sites for further development.[[337]](#cite_note-340) In February 2011, the state passed legislation permitting the construction of [windmills](/wiki/Windmills "Windmills") for electricity along pre-existing piers, such as the [Steel Pier](/wiki/Steel_Pier "Steel Pier").[[338]](#cite_note-341)[[339]](#cite_note-342) The first phase of the [Atlantic Wind Connection](/wiki/Atlantic_Wind_Connection "Atlantic Wind Connection"), a planned [electrical transmission backbone](/wiki/Electric_power_transmission "Electric power transmission") along the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore") was planned to be operational in 2013.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Further information: [Category:Films shot in Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Category:Films_shot_in_Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Category:Films shot in Atlantic City, New Jersey")

Atlantic City is featured prominently in films, television, and music, including:

* **Films**: *[The King of Marvin Gardens](/wiki/The_King_of_Marvin_Gardens "The King of Marvin Gardens")* (1972),[[340]](#cite_note-343) *[Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City_(1980_film) "Atlantic City (1980 film)")* (1980),[[341]](#cite_note-344) *[The Godfather Part III](/wiki/The_Godfather_Part_III "The Godfather Part III")* (1990),[[342]](#cite_note-345) *[Rounders](/wiki/Rounders_(film) "Rounders (film)")* (1998),[[343]](#cite_note-346) and *[Snake Eyes](/wiki/Snake_Eyes_(1998_film) "Snake Eyes (1998 film)")* (1998).[[344]](#cite_note-347)
* **Television**: *[Mr. Belvedere](/wiki/Mr._Belvedere "Mr. Belvedere")*, *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*,[[345]](#cite_note-348) *[How I Met Your Mother](/wiki/How_I_Met_Your_Mother "How I Met Your Mother")*,[[346]](#cite_note-349) *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")*, and *[Boardwalk Empire](/wiki/Boardwalk_Empire "Boardwalk Empire")*.[[347]](#cite_note-ACNJ-empire-350)
* **Music**: Atlantic City is lyrically mentioned or the primary subject of *[On the Boardwalk (In Atlantic City)](/wiki/On_the_Boardwalk_(In_Atlantic_City) "On the Boardwalk (In Atlantic City)")* by [June Haver](/wiki/June_Haver "June Haver") (1946)[[348]](#cite_note-351) and "[Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City_(song) "Atlantic City (song)")" by [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") (1982).

* [Myron Goldfinger](/wiki/Myron_Goldfinger "Myron Goldfinger") (1933–2023), architect best known for designing large angular abstract houses in New York and New Jersey[[391]](#cite_note-394)
* [William Green](/wiki/William_Green_(American_football) "William Green (American football)") (born 1979), NFL [running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") who played for the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns")[[392]](#cite_note-395)
* [Marjorie Guthrie](/wiki/Marjorie_Guthrie "Marjorie Guthrie") (1917–1983), dancer of the Martha Graham Company and dance teacher who was the wife of folk musician [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie")[[393]](#cite_note-396)
* [Cornelia Hancock](/wiki/Cornelia_Hancock "Cornelia Hancock") (1840–1927), volunteer [nurse](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing") who served the injured and infirmed of the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")[[394]](#cite_note-397)
* [John R. Hargrove Sr.](/wiki/John_R._Hargrove_Sr. "John R. Hargrove Sr.") (1923–1997), federal judge appointed by President Ronald Reagan to the [United States District Court for the District of Maryland](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Maryland "United States District Court for the District of Maryland")[[395]](#cite_note-398)
* [Celestine Tate Harrington](/wiki/Celestine_Tate_Harrington "Celestine Tate Harrington") (1956–1998), quadriplegic street musician known for playing keyboard with her lips, teeth and tongue on the Atlantic City boardwalk[[396]](#cite_note-399)
* [James Hillman](/wiki/James_Hillman "James Hillman") (1926–2011), developer of [archetypal psychology](/wiki/Archetypal_psychology "Archetypal psychology")[[397]](#cite_note-400)
* [Pete Hunter](/wiki/Pete_Hunter "Pete Hunter") (born 1980), [cornerback](/wiki/Cornerback "Cornerback") who played in the NFL for the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks")[[398]](#cite_note-401)
* [Walter S. Jeffries](/wiki/Walter_S._Jeffries "Walter S. Jeffries") (1893–1954), represented [New Jersey's 2nd congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_congressional_district "New Jersey's 2nd congressional district") from 1939 to 1941[[399]](#cite_note-402)
* [Candy Jones](/wiki/Candy_Jones "Candy Jones") (1925–1990), fashion model, writer and radio talk show host[[400]](#cite_note-403)
* [Marvin Josephson](/wiki/Marvin_Josephson "Marvin Josephson") (1927–2022), [talent agent](/wiki/Talent_agent "Talent agent") and executive, who was the founder of [ICM Partners](/wiki/ICM_Partners "ICM Partners")[[401]](#cite_note-404)
* [Allan Kaprow](/wiki/Allan_Kaprow "Allan Kaprow") (1927–2006), painter and pioneer in establishing concepts of [performance art](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art"), who influenced [Fluxus](/wiki/Fluxus "Fluxus")[[402]](#cite_note-405)
* [Amy Kennedy](/wiki/Amy_Kennedy "Amy Kennedy") (born 1978), educator, mental health advocate and politician who was the [Democratic-Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") nominee in the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections_in_New_Jersey "2020 United States House of Representatives elections in New Jersey") to represent [New Jersey's 2nd congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_congressional_district "New Jersey's 2nd congressional district")[[403]](#cite_note-406)
* [Marie Kibler](/wiki/Marie_Kibler "Marie Kibler") (1912–1978), [artistic gymnast](/wiki/Artistic_gymnastics "Artistic gymnastics") who competed at the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics") and placed fifth with the American team[[404]](#cite_note-407)
* [Pinky Kravitz](/wiki/Pinky_Kravitz "Pinky Kravitz") (1927–2015), radio broadcaster and print journalist who hosted "Pinky's Corner" on [WOND](/wiki/WOND "WOND") from an array of Atlantic City locations from 1958 until a few months before his death in 2015, hosted "WMGM presents Pinky!" for years on [WMGM-TV](/wiki/WMGM-TV "WMGM-TV") and wrote columns for many periodicals including *[The Press of Atlantic City](/wiki/The_Press_of_Atlantic_City "The Press of Atlantic City")*[[405]](#cite_note-408)
* [Martha Krebs](/wiki/Martha_Krebs "Martha Krebs"), [theoretical physicist](/wiki/Theoretical_physicists "Theoretical physicists") who directed the [Office of Science and Technology Policy](/wiki/Office_of_Science_and_Technology_Policy "Office of Science and Technology Policy") for the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") and is the founding director for the [California NanoSystems Institute](/wiki/California_NanoSystems_Institute "California NanoSystems Institute") at [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles")[[406]](#cite_note-409)
* [JoAnna LaSane](/wiki/JoAnna_LaSane "JoAnna LaSane") (1935–2019), model, dancer and arts administrator[[407]](#cite_note-410)
* [Lee B. Laskin](/wiki/Lee_B._Laskin "Lee B. Laskin") (1936–2024), attorney, politician and judge who served in both houses of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") before being appointed to serve on the [New Jersey Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court")[[408]](#cite_note-411)
* [Jacob Lawrence](/wiki/Jacob_Lawrence "Jacob Lawrence") (1917–2000), artist known for depicting African-American life in his paintings. Born on Arctic Avenue[[409]](#cite_note-412)[[410]](#cite_note-413)
* [E. Grey Lewis](/wiki/E._Grey_Lewis "E. Grey Lewis") (1940–2005), lawyer who served as [General Counsel of the Navy](/wiki/General_Counsel_of_the_Navy "General Counsel of the Navy")[[411]](#cite_note-414)
* [Bill Libby](/wiki/Bill_Libby "Bill Libby") (1927–1984), sportswriter and biographer best known for his books on sports, including 65 on sports figures[[412]](#cite_note-415)
* Valerie Mack, identified as a victim in the [Gilgo Beach serial killings](/wiki/Gilgo_Beach_serial_killings "Gilgo Beach serial killings")[[413]](#cite_note-416)

McCullough") (born 1942), politician who served in the [New Jersey Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") from 2007 to 2008, where he represented the [2nd Legislative District](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_legislative_district "New Jersey's 2nd legislative district")[[414]](#cite_note-417)
* [Don McGahn](/wiki/Don_McGahn "Don McGahn") (born 1968), [White House Counsel](/wiki/White_House_Counsel "White House Counsel") and Assistant to the President for U.S. President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and a former Commissioner of the United States [Federal Election Commission](/wiki/Federal_Election_Commission "Federal Election Commission")[[415]](#cite_note-418)
* [Bob Merrill](/wiki/Bob_Merrill "Bob Merrill") (1921–1998), songwriter and screenwriter[[416]](#cite_note-419)
* [Arnold Newman](/wiki/Arnold_Newman "Arnold Newman") (1918–2006), [photographer](/wiki/Photography "Photography"), noted for his "environmental portraits" of artists and politicians[[417]](#cite_note-420)
* [John P. O'Neill](/wiki/John_P._O%27Neill "John P. O'Neill") (1952–2001), FBI terrorist specialist who was director of security at the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") and died in the [September 11th, 2001 terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11th,_2001_terrorist_attacks "September 11th, 2001 terrorist attacks")[[418]](#cite_note-421)
* [Joshua Ozersky](/wiki/Joshua_Ozersky "Joshua Ozersky") (1967–2015), food writer and historian[[419]](#cite_note-422)
* [Chris Pallies](/wiki/King_Kong_Bundy "King Kong Bundy") (1957–2019), [professional wrestler](/wiki/Professional_wrestler "Professional wrestler") known as *[King Kong Bundy](/wiki/King_Kong_Bundy "King Kong Bundy")*[[420]](#cite_note-423)
* [Reese Palley](/wiki/Reese_Palley "Reese Palley") (1922–2015), "Merchant to the Rich", entrepreneur, art dealer, writer, and sailor[[421]](#cite_note-424)
* [Joseph B. Perskie](/wiki/Joseph_B._Perskie "Joseph B. Perskie") (1885–1957), Associate Justice of the [New Jersey Supreme Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Supreme_Court "New Jersey Supreme Court") from 1933 to 1947[[422]](#cite_note-425)
* [Jacqueline Reses](/wiki/Jacqueline_Reses "Jacqueline Reses"), businesswoman, investor, author and philanthropist[[423]](#cite_note-426)
* [Monique Samuels](/wiki/Monique_Samuels "Monique Samuels"), television personality best known as a cast member of the reality television series *[The Real Housewives of Potomac](/wiki/The_Real_Housewives_of_Potomac "The Real Housewives of Potomac")*[[424]](#cite_note-427)
* [Alfredo Silipigni](/wiki/Alfredo_Silipigni "Alfredo Silipigni") (1932–2006), conductor and founder of [New Jersey State Opera](/wiki/New_Jersey_State_Opera "New Jersey State Opera")[[425]](#cite_note-428)
* [Jeremy Slate](/wiki/Jeremy_Slate "Jeremy Slate") (1926–2006), actor and songwriter[[426]](#cite_note-429)
* [George Smathers](/wiki/George_Smathers "George Smathers") (1913–2007), [United States Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator") from Florida[[427]](#cite_note-430)
* [Larry Steele](/wiki/Larry_Steele_(producer) "Larry Steele (producer)") (1913–1980), [impresario](/wiki/Impresario "Impresario") known for his [revues](/wiki/Revue "Revue") with black casts, based in [Club Harlem](/wiki/Club_Harlem "Club Harlem")[[428]](#cite_note-431)
* [Dave Thomas](/wiki/Dave_Thomas_(American_businessman) "Dave Thomas (American businessman)") (1932–2002), founder of [Wendy's](/wiki/Wendy%27s "Wendy's") fast-food restaurant, was born in Atlantic City[[429]](#cite_note-432)
* [Jean Webster](/wiki/Jean_Webster_(cook) "Jean Webster (cook)") (1935–2011), cook who operated Sister Jean's Kitchen, a soup kitchen serving free meals to the poor of Atlantic City[[430]](#cite_note-433)
* [Jim Whelan](/wiki/Jim_Whelan "Jim Whelan") (1948–2017), member of [New Jersey Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") who represented the [2nd Legislative District](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_2nd_legislative_district "New Jersey's 2nd legislative district") until his death and was Mayor of Atlantic City from 1990 to 2001[[431]](#cite_note-434)
* [Norman Joseph Woodland](/wiki/Norman_Joseph_Woodland "Norman Joseph Woodland") (1921–2012), inventor of the [barcode](/wiki/Barcode "Barcode")[[432]](#cite_note-435)
* [Albert Zugsmith](/wiki/Albert_Zugsmith "Albert Zugsmith") (1910–1993), film producer[[433]](#cite_note-436)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-100)** Mean monthly maxima and minima (i.e. the highest and lowest temperature readings during an entire month or year) calculated based on data at said location from 1991 to 2020.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-102)** The official climatology station for Atlantic City was at the Weather Bureau Office downtown from January 1874 to 15 June 1958 and Atlantic City Int'l (ACY) in [Egg Harbor Township](/wiki/Egg_Harbor_Township "Egg Harbor Township") since 16 June 1958.[[100]](#cite_note-101) ACY's location in the [Pine Barrens](/wiki/Pine_Barrens_(New_Jersey) "Pine Barrens (New Jersey)") and distance away from the coast and [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") of downtown Atlantic City largely account for its markedly colder temperatures at night as compared to downtown; for example, from 1959 to 2013, there were 50 days with a low of 0 °F (−18 °C) or lower, while in the same period, the corresponding number of days at downtown was 2. The [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service") ceased regular snowfall observations at downtown after the winter of 1958–59.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-popnote_224-0)** based on 2022 census data
